SinglefabricMulticastVXLAN(V4andV6)|527

L2 multicast over VXLAN enabled with IGMP or MLD snooping

IGMP or MLD snooping can be configured at the VLAN level with L2 multicast over VXLAN tunnels, this
will prevent the source connected VTEP from sending the multicast stream to VTEPs without any
interested receivers (non-receivers).

Figure 2 L2 multicast over VXLAN enabled with IGMP snooping

L2 multicast over VXLAN enabled with IGMP snooping has the following characteristics:

n IGMP or MLD snooping, once enabled prevents flooding over the L2 VNI; multicast data is only

forwarded to ports where IGMP or MLD joins are received.

n VTEPs have full meshed VXLAN tunnels between them.

n IGMP or MLD snooping is enabled per VLAN; the VLAN is mapped with L2 VNIs.

n Joins and leaves are learned over VTEPs.

n Packets (data and control) are sent over VXLAN tunnels and regular physical ports.

n All versions of IGMP or MLD snooping (v1/v2/v3) are supported over VXLAN.

AOS-CX 10.12 Multicast Guide | (6200, 6300, 6400, 8xxx, 9300, 10000 Switch Series)

528

n The querier can be inside or outside the VXLAN network.

n VSX is supported and can be L2 extended.

n When IGMP or MLD snooping is enabled on a VLAN (L2 VNI), all the tunnels are added as forwarded

ports (forwarded tunnels) for control packets; the control packets are flooded to all VTEPs.

n As the network is meshed, joins from a non-querier VTEP is sent to the source VTEP, even if is not the

querier.

n Multicast data packets are sent from source VTEP to remote VTEPs.

n Split horizon is implemented at the L2 level such that packets that ingress a VXLAN tunnel will not get

re-forwarded to other VTEPs.

Split horizon and L2 multicast

AOS-CX implements split horizon in VXLAN networks to prevent loops; ingressing traffic on a VXLAN
tunnel is not sent to another VXLAN tunnel. This can cause traffic loss to both unknown multicast and L2
multicast as IGMP or MLD joins are sent to querier. The querier typically is the middle device that
transmits packets from one port to another. In case of IGMP or MLD over an L2 VXLAN tunnel, this is
blocked by split horizon. To prevent this, the receiver-connected VTEP forwards IGMP or MLD joins to all
VTEPs irrespective of whether they are queriers or not. This prevents tunnel hop as every source VTEP is
only a single tunnel hop away from every receiver and source data is sent to the receiver VTEP directly
without the involvement of the IGMP or MLD querier.

VSX and L2 multicast

In the AOS-CX implementation, two switches/VTEPs configured with Virtual Switching Extension (VSX)
are configured as a single logical VTEP. AOS-CX does not use EVPN route type 1 and 4 for load balancing
and multihoming; regular VSX-based syncing is used for those purposes. The VSX switches can be
access or distribution/core VTEPs in a campus network, and are typically VSX leaf switches in a data
center.

There are two scenarios that need to be considered for an L2 VNI that is stretched across VTEPs where
one (or more) of the VTEPs have a VSX Link Aggregation Group (LAG).

n Scenario 1: Receivers connected to VSX LAG and source on the VXLAN overlay network.

n Scenario 2: Source connected to VSX LAG and receivers on the VXLAN overlay network.

Scenario 1: Receivers connected to VSX LAG and source on the VXLAN overlay network

There are two cases to consider: dual homed VSX LAG and single homed VSX LAG.

Dual-homed VSX LAG with receivers

Figure 3 Dual-homed VSX LAG with receivers

Single fabric Multicast VXLAN (V4 and V6) | 529

Thefollowingoperationisdoneinnoparticularorder:
n IGMPorMLD joinsfromreceiveroranaccessswitcharesyncedbetweentheVSXswitchesoverthe
ISLlink,asaresultbothVSXswitcheswilllearnthejoinfromtheVSXLAGlink.
n DatatrafficfromthemulticastsourcethroughtheVXLANtunnelreachesoneoftheVSXswitches.
n WhicheverVSXVTEPreceivesthetrafficforwardsitdownstream.
WhenoneoftheVSXswitchesisthequerier,thetrafficisforwardedtothequerieraswellviaInter
n
SwitchLink(ISL);otherwisethereisnotrafficontheISLlink.
n EgressfilteringrulepreventsingressdataonanISLportfromgoingdowntheVSXLAGifitismulti-
homed.
| Single-homed                            | VSX LAG with | receivers |
| --------------------------------------- | ------------ | --------- |
| Figure4 Single-homedVSXLAGwithreceivers |              |           |
530
| AOS-CX10.12MulticastGuide| | (6200,6300,6400,8xxx,9300,10000SwitchSeries) |     |
| -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | --- |

The following operation is done in no particular order:

n The IGMP or MLD joins from a receiver or an access switch are synced between the VSX switches

over the ISL link, as a result both the VSX switches learn the join over the VSX LAG link.

n The moment the VSX LAG becomes single homed, the ISL port is added as a flood port. This ensures

any data received by one of VSX VTEP is always forwarded to the peer

n Data traffic from the multicast source through the VXLAN tunnel reaches one of VSX switches and is

forwarded via ISL to its peer.

n Whichever VSX VTEP has its link up forwards the data downstream, either received directly or via ISL.

n As the VSX LAG is single-homed, there is no egress filtering that prevents data going from ISL to VSX

LAG.

Scenario 2: Source connected to VSX LAG and receivers on the VXLAN overlay network

Single fabric Multicast VXLAN (V4 and V6) | 531

As shown below, the source can be directly connected to the VSX VTEPs or it could be connected via an
L2 switch. In both the cases, data reaches one of the VSX VTEPs because of VSX hash.

Figure 5 Source connected to VSX LAG

The following sequence can happen in no particular order:

n One of the switches in the L2 network is elected as querier and unknown multicast are always sent to
the querier port. IGMP or MLD joins are always forwarded to VXLAN tunnels as well as to the querier
port.

n Data traffic from the multicast source reaches the VSX switches either because they are in the querier

path or because of the VSX LAG hash (in case directly connected),

n IGMP or MLD joins from receiver reaches one of the VSX switches. The joins are sent over ISL port to

the peer switch. The peer VSX switch does a MAC lookup to figure out whether the join is to be
learned on the ISL port or over the VXLAN tunnel.

n Once the join is learned on the VXLAN tunnel, multicast data is sent over the VXLAN tunnel when
received from the VSX LAG. The path is the same for both a single-homed and a dual-homed VSX
LAG.

Recommended configuration on the VSX VTEPs

As shown in Figure 5, Source connected to VSX LAG, the receiver-connected VTEP has a VXLAN tunnel to
the VSX logical VTEP. The joins from receiver-connected VTEP are received by one of the VSX switches
over the VXLAN tunnel and by the other switch via ISL. The VTEP that receives over ISL is a de-capsulated
packet and does not have the VXLAN header. That switch needs to perform a MAC lookup and that MAC
sometimes might not be available to the VSX VTEP.

To avoid this condition, the following two commands are recommended on L2 connected VSX VTEPs:

AOS-CX 10.12 Multicast Guide | (6200, 6300, 6400, 8xxx, 9300, 10000 Switch Series)

532

n redistribute local-mac

This command is used to enable Type-2 route advertisement for local MAC address of all EVPN
enabled VLANs.

n redistribute local-svi

This command is used to enable Type-2 route advertisement for local IP address and MAC address of
the SVI interfaces corresponding to the EVPN enabled VLANs (only required if SVI is configured).

IGMP or MLD querier positioning

An IGMP or MLD querier is one of the routers in the network whose responsibility is to send periodic
IGMP or MLD queries. In regular IGMP or MLD operation, IGMP or MLD joins are forwarded to the IGMP
or MLD querier. In addition, unknown multicast data is also sent to the querier. That is the way that the
querier connects source and destination routers. Sending joins towards the querier is a must for the
IGMP or MLD snooping operation; however in a VXLAN network, because of split-horizon rules, data
packets are not sent across VTEPs. The querier does not need to be a centralized router in a VXLAN
network as every IGMP or MLD join from the receiver connected VTEP is always forwarded to all remote
VTEPs with the same VNI. In the AOS-CX implementation, there is no particular recommendation on
where to place the querier in a VXLAN network.

L3 multicast over VXLAN

For L3 Multicast over VXLAN, both centralized L3 gateway and distributed L3 gateway use cases are
supported.

Centralized L3 gateway

As shown in Figure 6, Centralized L3 gateway, PIM-SM is only enabled at the centralized L3 gateway
VTEP which has VLAN mapped to VNIs. The remaining switches function as L2 VTEPs. The L2 VTEPs may
or may not have IGMP or MLD snooping enabled.

n This use case is simple to implement as PIM-SM is enabled only at the centralized L3 gateway VTEP.

n Mroute entries are simple as routing is between Switch Virtual Interfaces (SVI)/Routed Only Ports

(ROP) to L2 VNIs.

n The only difference is that the downstream VLANs are mapped to L2 VNIs; multicast data stream
packets are tunneled via L2 VNI from the centralized L3 gateway VTEP towards the L2 VTEPs.

Figure 6 Centralized L3 gateway

Single fabric Multicast VXLAN (V4 and V6) | 533

Distributed L3 gateways

n For distributed L3 gateways (See Figure 7, Distributed L3 Gateways), AOS-CX only supports

Symmetric IRB and multicast routing by extension of the PIM-SM protocol in the VXLAN tunnel. Only
PIM-SM protocol is supported with L3 multicast over VXLAN tunnels.

n An L3 VNI is used to route traffic from one VLAN to another as the same VLAN/L2 VNI is not present

across all VTEPs. PIM creates a logical interface on L3 VNI when router pim is enabled on the overlay
VRF. This interface is created once the VXLAN EVPN tunnels are formed.

n All VTEPs create their per-tenant VRF L3 VNI interface and exchange PIM messages (e.g. hellos and
joins) to other VTEPs through it. They form PIM neighbors via this interface; when the remote VTEP
requests traffic via PIM joins on this interface on the source-connected VTEP, a multicast route entry
is installed with the incoming interface as SVI interface (source-connected SVI) and Outgoing Interface
List (OIL) with an L3 VNI<#> interface.

n On the receiver connected VTEP, a multicast route entry is created to route traffic coming from L3

VNI to the SVI where the receivers are present. Incoming interface is L3 VNI#, with OIL as the receiver
SVI.

Figure 7 Distributed L3 Gateways

AOS-CX 10.12 Multicast Guide | (6200, 6300, 6400, 8xxx, 9300, 10000 Switch Series)

534

Additional details include the following:

n Symmetric IRB with full meshed L3 VNIs.

n L2 VNIs can extended across switches.

n IGMP or MLD joins may be present in some/all the VTEPs for a particular flow.

n IGMP or MLD snooping will be enabled in VTEPs.

n PIM-SM is enabled on all the VRFs on the different VTEPs.

n PIM neighbors are formed across L3 VNIs.

n RPs can be present on any of the VTEPs.

n PIM control (join and prune) packets are sent on the other side of the L3 VNIs (where source is

present) for every IGMP or MLD join.

n When the source connected VTEP sees a PIM join from an L3 VNI, it adds the L3 VNI as OIL.

n Data traffic from the multicast source is flooded across L3VNIs, split horizon rules will prevent data

from flooding across VTEPs (see Figure 8, Distributed L3 Gateways with non-receiver, multicast data
stream sent and pruned at remote VTEP).

n Non-interested VTEPs will drop/prune the multicast data (see Figure 8, Distributed L3 Gateways with

non-receiver, multicast data stream sent and pruned at remote VTEP).

n In mixed mode there could be some L2VNIs that will be extended over VTEPS whereas L3 VNIs are

enabled in all the VTEPs (per VRF).

n Mix of IGMP or MLD snooping and PIM-SM for intra VLAN and inter VLAN routing.

Figure 8 Distributed L3 Gateways with non-receiver, multicast data stream sent and pruned at remote VTEP

Single fabric Multicast VXLAN (V4 and V6) | 535

IncasetherearenointerestedreceiversatremoteVTEPs,L3VNIarenotaddedasanoutgoinginterface
anddataareprunedatthesource-connectedVTEP(See Figure9,DistributedL3Gatewayswithnon-
receivers,multicastdatastreamprunedatsourceVTEP).
Figure9 DistributedL3Gatewayswithnon-receivers,multicastdatastreamprunedatsourceVTEP
| Border VTEP | to external | (non overlay) | network |
| ----------- | ----------- | ------------- | ------- |
AborderVTEPcanbeusedtoconnecttheVXLANoverlaynetworktoexternal(nonoverlay)networksvia
eitherL2orL3,sourcesandreceiversinbothnetworksareabletosendandreceivemulticaststreams.
L3multicastroutingusingPIM-SMbetweentheVXLANandexternal(nonoverlay)networkis
recommendedformostdeploymentsasthisprovidescleardemarcationbetweenthetwonetworks.
| Figure10 BorderVTEPtoexternal(nonoverlay)network |     |     |     |
| ------------------------------------------------ | --- | --- | --- |
536
| AOS-CX10.12MulticastGuide| | (6200,6300,6400,8xxx,9300,10000SwitchSeries) |     |     |
| -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | --- | --- |

VSX and L3 Multicast

The following actions are done for all VSX VTEPs with PIM-SM and/or IGMP or MLD enabled:

n The control packets (PIM packets) are synced between the VSX switches

n Unknown multicast packets are also synced between the VSX switches

n One of the VSX switches forwards the packets to prevent duplicates

There are different combinations with VSX, with source inside the VXLAN overlay network and receivers
on external (non overlay) network and vice versa. The different ways a receiver or a source is connected
to a VSX border VTEP are:

n L2 VSX LAG

n L2 VSX LAG with SVIs

n ROP

n Point-to-Point (P2P) SVIs

VSX Border VTEP with L3 connectivity to external (non overlay) network

Figure 11, VSX Border VTEP with VSX LAG to receiver on external (non overlay) network shows a border
VTEP with VSX LAG and SVI connected to an L3 switch with receivers on the external (non overlay)
network.

Figure 11 VSX Border VTEP with VSX LAG to receiver on external (non overlay) network

Single fabric Multicast VXLAN (V4 and V6) | 537

The following actions are taken:

1. PIM hello packets are synced between the VSX border VTEPs via L3 over ISL.

2. PIM joins are received by both VSX switches from the VSX LAG with SVI, both switches send PIM

joins to the source-connected VTEP via L3 VNI.

3. The source-connected VTEP adds L3 VNI as the OIL even if it gets two PIM joins.

4. Data traffic from the multicast source is hashed from the source-connected VTEP to only one of

the VSX switches as a unicast packet over the VXLAN tunnel.

5. The VSX switch that receives the multicast data stream forms the Mroute entry with the SVI

connected to VSX LAG as egress.

6. The data stream packet goes to the other switch via ISL.

7. The egress filtering rule prevents duplicates as the second switch will have a bridge entry for the

egress SVI.

8. The external L3 Switch routes to the receiver.

9. A similar action occurs for a single-homed border VTEP with VSX LAG.

VSX Border VTEP with VSX LAG to receiver on external (non overlay) network shows a border VTEP with
VSX LAG and SVI connected to an L3 switch with source on the external (non overlay) network.

Figure 12 VSX Border VTEP with VSX LAG to source on external (non overlay) network

AOS-CX 10.12 Multicast Guide | (6200, 6300, 6400, 8xxx, 9300, 10000 Switch Series)

538

The following actions are taken:

1. PIM joins from the receiver-connected VTEP is only sent to one of the VSX border VTEP, this is

synced to the other VSX peer.

2. PIM hello packets are synced between the VSX switches via L3 over ISL.

3. Both VSX border VTEPs that receive the PIM join adds L3 VNI as OIL.

4. Multicast data stream from the external network reaches both VSX border VTEPs via VSX LAG with

SVI followed by ISL.

5. The primary VSX switch forwards the data to receiver-connected remote VTEP (applies to the

Aruba 8325, 8400, 9300, and 10000 Switch Series).

6. When 1 of the VSX border VTEP gets data directly and not via ISL, it forwards to the receiver-

connected remote VTEP (applies to the Aruba 6400, 8100, and 8360 Switch Series).

VSX Border VTEP with ROP/P2P SVIs to source on external (non overlay) network shows a border VTEP
with ROP/P2P SVIs connected to an L3 switch with source on the external (non overlay) network.

Figure 13 VSX Border VTEP with ROP/P2P SVIs to source on external (non overlay) network

Single fabric Multicast VXLAN (V4 and V6) | 539

The following actions are taken:

1. PIM hello packets are synced between the VSX VTEPs via L3 over ISL.

2. Both VTEPs send PIM joins to the external L3 switch via ROPs; the assumption is that the source

has Equal Cost Multi Pathing (ECMP) to both VTEPs.

3. The external L3 switch adds two ROPs/P2P SVIs as OIL in its Mroute entry.

4. Multicast data is sent to both border VTEPs.

5. 8325/8400—only VSX primary VTEP forwards.

6. 6400/8100/8360—only the VTEP that has pending joins forwards.

7.

If the link from primary VTEP to the source goes down and primary becomes unreachable, then
there can be traffic loss. In such a scenario, it is recommended to connect an additional L3 link
per tenant VRF and enable PIM over them. This link is typically a P2P SVI between the VSX peers.
Each tenant VRF requires its own P2P SVI. The ip pim-sparse vsx virtual-neighbor command
should not be configured on this P2P SVI.

A. VSX Border VTEP with ROP/P2P SVIs to receivers on external (non-overlay) network and B. VSX Border
VTEP with ROP/P2P SVIs to receivers on external (non-overlay) network show a border VTEP with
ROP/P2P SVIs connected to an L3 switch with receivers on the external (non overlay) network. The
example in Figure 16 shows multicast data and joins sent to different VSX switches, and the example in
B. VSX Border VTEP with ROP/P2P SVIs to receivers on external (non-overlay) network shows multicast
data and joins sent to the same VSX switch.

Figure 14 A. VSX Border VTEP with ROP/P2P SVIs to receivers on external (non-overlay) network

AOS-CX 10.12 Multicast Guide | (6200, 6300, 6400, 8xxx, 9300, 10000 Switch Series)

540

Figure 15 B. VSX Border VTEP with ROP/P2P SVIs to receivers on external (non-overlay) network

The following actions are taken:

1. PIM hello packets are synced between VSX switches via L3 over ISL.

2. PIM joins from ROP/P2P will reach one of the VSX switches.

3. Only one VSX switch sends the PIM join towards the source-connected VTEP.

Single fabric Multicast VXLAN (V4 and V6) | 541

4. Thesource-connectedVTEPonlyaddstheL3VNIasanOILandforwardmulticastdatatotheL3
VNI.
5. AnyVSXswitchthatreceivesdatafromtheL3VNIaddsthededicatedL3Interface(asanOILfor
alldatacomingfromL3VNI)andpendingjoinedportasegressinterface.
6. TheotherVSXswitchreceivesdataoverthededicatedL3Interface.ThatVSXswitchaddsany
pendingROP/P2Pjoinsasegressinterface.NoSVIisaddedasOILastheVSXswitchwhich
receivedthedatafromtheL3VNIwouldhavealreadyaddedtheSVItoOIL.

| EVPN | VXLAN | distributed | anycast | gateway |
| ---- | ----- | ----------- | ------- | ------- |
ThedistributedanycastgatewayfeatureforEVPNVXLANisadefaultgateway-addressingmechanism
thatenablestheuseofthesamegatewayIPaddressesacrossalltheVTEPswitchesthatarepartofa
VXLANnetwork.
ForPIMv6,boththeIPv6link-localandtheactive-gatewayaddressoftheanycastVLANinterfaceareconfigured
withtheanycastlink-localaddress.
Intheanycastscenario,allthreeaddress(primary,secondary,andactivegateway)arethesame.

Inthisanycastgatewaysolution,theVXLANsourceIPaddressisusedasthedesignatedrouter(DR)
priorityanditoverridesthedefaultDRpriority.
Theshow ip pim interface <vlan-id>commandshowstheoperationalDRpriorityandalsotheDR
priorityoftheelectedDR.Whendistributedanycastgatewayisconfigured,allthePIMneighborsshare
thesameanycastIP.TheDRelectionispossibleherebyinternallyassigningauniqueDRpriorityonthe
anycastinterfacesandismanagedbyPIM.AllneighborsintheanycastsegmentgetauniqueDRpriority
thatcanbeviewedintheOperationalDRPriorityfield.TheElectedDRinthesegmentcanbeidentified
bytheElectedDRPriorityfield.
Theshow ip pim neighbor all-vrfscommandalsodisplayswhetherananycastneighborispresent.
Recommendations
n Forseamlessoperation,itisrecommendednottomanuallyconfigureDRprioritieswhenanycast
gatewayisconfigured.
n IftheDRprioritiesareconfigured,theyshouldbeuniqueineachoftheleafnodesandneedtohave
thesamevalueontheVSXpeers.
Limitation
AnycastVLANscannotbeconfiguredasacandidate-RPorcandidate-BSR.Buttheycanlearntheelected
RPsetthroughBSR.

Considerations and best practices
The following considerations and best practices are recommended for Multicast VXLAN:

n IGMP or MLD snooping should be enabled on all VTEPs; multicast traffic flooding will happen without

IGMP or MLD snooping.

n redistribute local-svi or redistribute local-mac are required on all VTEPs for proper querier

information propagation.

nIn a VSX VxLAN deployment, ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor needs to be enabled on all PIM
enabled SVI interfaces with VSX-LAGs and L2 VNI mapped SVI interfaces. This is needed for both L2 and L3
extensions.

n A unique virtual-mac should be configured on all VTEPs for a distributed L3 gateway use case; a VSX

VTEP pair should be configured with the same virtual-mac.

n RP can be placed in the overlay network or in the external non-overlay network. Any existing RP in
the external non-overlay network can also be used. It is recommended for RPs to be placed near
multicast sources.

n Underlay links, which is used to establish overlay connectivity, should not be enabled with multicast

(PIM/IGMP/MLD) configuration.

n Overlay RPs or BSRs on VSX VTEPs are only supported on the Aruba 6400, 8100, 8325, 8360, 9300 and

8400 Switch Series.

n It is not recommended to enable ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor on P2P SVI or ROP extensions.

Enabling the command can lead to traffic loss.

n Customers who need to route Multicast Traffic across VRF's, need to configure the VRF merging

router as the RP/BSR in the overlay networks.

These are the supported VSX VTEP use cases:

Single fabric Multicast VXLAN (V4 and V6) | 549

n VSXswitches(logicalVTEPs)forIGMPorMLDsnoopingandPIM-SM
n VSXborderVTEPwithVSXLAG,L2extensiontosources/receiversinexternal/nonoverlaynetwork
VSXborderVTEPwithVSXLAG,SVI+PIM-SML3extensiontosources/receiversinexternal/non
n
overlaynetwork
n VSXborderVTEPwithROP/P2PSVIs+PIM-SML3extensiontosourcesandreceiversinexternal/non
overlaynetwork
ConsiderthefollowingwhendeployingROPandP2PSVIasuplinksonVSXborderVTEPstonon-VXLAN
networks:
n AnadditionalL3peeringpertenantVRFbetweenVSXprimaryandsecondaryonoverlayisrequired
tohandlefailoverscenarios,thislinkwillnormallybeP2PSVIsoverISL.
o ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-includecommandisrequiredtobeconfiguredontheL3
peering,whenreceiversareconnectedbehindROP orP2PSVIuplinks.
Forexample:
hostname BorderVTEP1A
|     | interface | vlan        | 1500         |     |
| --- | --------- | ----------- | ------------ | --- |
|     |           | description | transit VLAN |     |
|     |           | vrf attach  | VRF1         |     |
! IPv4
|     |     | ip address     | 1.1.2.1/30            |     |
| --- | --- | -------------- | --------------------- | --- |
|     |     | ip ospf 1 area | 0.0.0.0               |     |
|     |     | ip pim-sparse  | enable                |     |
|     |     | ip pim-sparse  | datapath-auto-include |     |
n WhenusingaP2PSVIL3extension,donotallowupstreamVLANsinISL;itwillmakethelinknonP2P.
n ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighborcommandisnotrecommendedtobeenabledonthe
additionalL3peeringorP2PSVIlinkpertenantVRFbetweenVSXpeers.
ThefollowingrestrictionsareapplicableforIPv4MulticastVXLAN:
n MSDP,mDNS,mtrace,InterVRFRouteLeaking(IVRL),andPIM-DMoverVXLANarenotsupported.
n MulticastoverstaticVXLANtunnelsarenotsupported.
n GShutisnotsupportedonVXLANbasedoverlaynetworks.

towards the non-VXLAN network (as mentioned in the Considerations and best practices section).

ip pim-sparse enable
ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor

interface vlan 1008

vsx-sync active-gateways
ip address 101.8.1.253/24
active-gateway ip mac 00:00:20:00:10:01
active-gateway ip 101.8.1.254
ip igmp enable
ip pim-sparse enable
ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor

interface vlan 1009

vsx-sync active-gateways
ip address 101.9.1.253/24
active-gateway ip mac 00:00:20:00:10:01
active-gateway ip 101.9.1.254
ip igmp enable
ip pim-sparse enable
ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor

interface vlan 1010

ip address 101.10.1.253/24
active-gateway ip mac 00:00:20:00:10:01
active-gateway ip 101.10.1.254
ip igmp enable
ip pim-sparse enable
ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor

interface vxlan 1

vxlan-counters aggregate
no shutdown

vlan 1001

Use Case 3: IPv6 VXLAN tunnel source with dual stack IPv4/IPv6
| multicast overlay |     |     |
| ----------------- | --- | --- |
PIMsupportsbothIPv4andIPv6asunderlayVTEPIPaddresses.IndeploymentswithanIPv6underlay,
PIMformsanauto-generatedlink-localaddresstoexchangecontrolpacketsandformsaPIM
neighborshipoveranL3VNIinterfacewithotherVTEPs.TheautogeneratedIPwillbealinklocalIPin
caseofoverlayIPv6.
ThefollowingcommandintroducedinAOS-CX10.12.1000toconfigureanIPaddressforL3VNI
neighborship.
| ipv6 multicast | pim6 l3vni-source-ip | X:X::X:X |
| -------------- | -------------------- | -------- |
Thisconfigurationprovidesgreaternetworkmanageabilitybyoverridingtheauto-generatedIPv6
addresswithauser-specifiedIPv6 addressforthePIML3VNIinterface.Thisconfigurationisoptional
andonlyapplicablefordeploymentsusinganIPv6underlaytype.TheIPv6addressconfiguredwiththis
commandwillbeusedonlyforPIMcontrolpacketexchangesandwillnotresideintherouter's
forwardingtables.
Figure1 IPv6VXLANtunnelsourcewithdualstackIPv4/IPv6multicastoverlay

Verifying the configuration
SinglefabricMulticastVXLAN(V4andV6)|723

Use the following procedure to verify the correct configuration of a IPv6 VXLAN tunnel source with dual
stack IPv4/IPv6 multicast overlay, as shown in Use Case#3.

1. Verify the Access VTEP configuration.

a. Verify ospfv3 output on the 6300 VSF VTEP switch using the show ipv6 ospfv3 neighbors

command.

b. Verify BGP neighborship on the 6300 VSF VTEP switch using the command show bgp all

summary.

2. Verify IPv6 VXLAN tunnels are operational.

a. On the 6400 access switch, verify L2VNI tunnels and L3VNI tunnels are UP using the show

interface vxlan vteps command.

AOS-CX 10.12 Multicast Guide | (6200, 6300, 6400, 8xxx, 9300, 10000 Switch Series)

724

b. On the 6400 access switch, verify PIM6 neighbors are UP over vxlan for each VRF using the

show ipv6 pim6 neighbor brief vrf <vrf> command.

Single fabric Multicast VXLAN (V4 and V6) | 725

3. Verify MLD joins are acting expected for all VLANs using the show ipv6 mld snooping vlan
<vlan>and show ipv6 mld interface <interface> commands on the 6400 access switch.

AOS-CX 10.12 Multicast Guide | (6200, 6300, 6400, 8xxx, 9300, 10000 Switch Series)

726

4. Verify pim6 mroute using the show ipv6 mld mrout vrf <vrf> command on the 6400 access

switch.

5. Verify RP-set info is learned on all VTEPs.

a.

Issue the show ipv6 pim6 rp-set vrf <vrf> command on the 6300 standalone VTEP switch.

Single fabric Multicast VXLAN (V4 and V6) | 727

b. Issuetheshow ipv6 pim6 rp-set vrf all-vrfscommandonthe6300VTEPVSFswitch.
| Example | Configuration | Outputs |
| ------- | ------------- | ------- |

accessswitch.

Description
EnablestheroutertoforwardmulticastdatareceivedontheVXLAN L3VNIfabrictothisinterface,
regardlessofwhetheramulticastjoinwasreceivedonthisinterfaceornot.Thisallowstheinterfaceto
beinthesamemulticastdatapathstateonboththeVSXpeers.
ThiscommandmustbeenabledontheVSX VXLANrouterstosupportmulticastreceiversoverROP or
P2P SVIextensions.ThiscommandcanbeenabledonatransitL3peeringbetweenVSX peers.This
commandisoptionalwhentheuplinkisanMCLAGSVI.
Thenoformofthecommanddisablesforwardingofmulticastdataontheinterface.
n AnIP addressmustbeconfiguredontheinterfaceandpim-sparsemustbeenabled.
n ThiscommandmustbeenabledonlyononeinterfaceperVRF.
PIMenabledVXLANVTEPscanbeextendedtootherroutersthatcanbeconnectedtosourcesorclients.
ThefollowingtypesofL3orL2extensionsaresupported:
n L2VSXLAG:UpstreamordownstreamroutersareconnectedusingL2VSXLAGlinks.
n L3VSXLAG:UpstreamordownstreamroutersareconnectedusingL3VSXLAGlinks.
n ROPextension:L3extensionforsourcesorclientsusingROPsissupported.
Point-to-pointSVIextension:L3extensionforsourcesorclientsusingpoint-to-pointSVIsis
n
supported.
IfthesourceisconnectedusingROPorP2PSVIs,itisrecommendedtohaveanadditionalL3linkper
VRFbetweentheVSXdevicesforupstreamredundancy.IftheL3linkisanSVI,itisrecommendedtonot
addanISLportinthatVLAN.
SinglefabricMulticastVXLAN(V4andV6)|733

datapath-auto-include Includestheinterfaceformulticastdataforwarding.

Description
Onceconfigured,therouterprocessesIGMP/MLDandPIMjoinsreceivedonthisinterfaceregardlessof
itsDRorPrimeNeighborrole.ThecommandmustbeenabledforVSXVXLANleafswitchesforbothL2
andL3extensions.Thisallowsfortheinterfacetobeinthesamemulticastdatapathstateonboththe
VSXpeers.
Thenoformofthecommanddisablesthevsx-virtual-neighborontheinterface.
ThiscommandisapplicablefornormalSVIinterfacesandL2VNImappedSVIinterfaces.ItisvalidforVXLAN-
enabledVLANsonlyandhasnoeffectonnon-VXLAN-enabledVLANs.

register-source
| register-source    | <INTERFACE-NAME> |     |     |
| ------------------ | ---------------- | --- | --- |
| no register-source | <INTERFACE-NAME> |     |     |
Description
SpecifiesthesourceinterfacetobeusedforPIMregistrationinthecaseofVXLANanycastinterfaces.
WhenthePIMenabledanycastVLANisdirectlyconnectedtoamulticastsource,register-sourceis
usedtosendregistrationmessagestotheRPandthisinterfacereceivestheregister-stopmessages
fromtheRP.
Thenoformofthiscommandremovestheregistersourceconfiguration.
| Parameter |     |     | Description |
| --------- | --- | --- | ----------- |
<INTERFACE-NAME>
Specifiesthenameoftheinterfacetouse.
Usage
n Thisisaglobalconfigurationunderrouter-pimconfigurationandisrequiredinSymmetricIRBwith
anycastIPaddressconfiguration.
n ThisconfigurationisrequiredinthesourceconnectedswitchonlywhenthePIM-DRandRParein
twodifferentswitches.
n Withoutthisconfiguration,therewillbetrafficlossastheregistrationsequencewillnotbe
successful.Itismandatorytohavethissourceinterfaceconfiguredwithanon-anycastIPaddress
whichisuniquetotheVTEP,andwithPIMenabled.
Examples
ConfiguringthesourceinterfaceforPIMregistrations:
SinglefabricMulticastVXLAN(V4andV6)|737

| switch# | config |     |     |
| ------- | ------ | --- | --- |
switch(config)#
|                     | router | pim vrf         | vrf1      |
| ------------------- | ------ | --------------- | --------- |
| switch(config-pim)# |        | register-source | loopback1 |
Removingtheregister-sourceconfiguration:
| switch(config-pim)# |         | no register-source | loopback1         |
| ------------------- | ------- | ------------------ | ----------------- |
| Command History     |         |                    |                   |
| Release             |         |                    | Modification      |
| 10.09.1000          |         |                    | Commandintroduced |
| Command Information |         |                    |                   |
| Platforms           | Command | context            | Authority         |
config-pim

havetheVSXconfigurationortheISLisdown,theoutputfromthe

switchesthatsupportVSX.

PIMsupportsbothIPv4andIPv6asunderlayVTEPIPaddresses.IndeploymentswithanIPv6underlay,
PIMformsanauto-generatedlink-localaddresstoexchangecontrolpacketsandformsaPIM
neighborshipoveranL3VNIinterfacewithotherVTEPs.TheautogeneratedIPwillbealinklocalIPin
caseofoverlayIPv6.
ThefollowingexampledisplaysL3VNIneighborsforanIPv6underlaytunnelwithanIPv4overlay.
| switch# | show ip pim | neighbor all-vrfs |     |     |
| ------- | ----------- | ----------------- | --- | --- |
PIM Neighbor
| VRF          |                 | : Test_1         |     |     |
| ------------ | --------------- | ---------------- | --- | --- |
| Total number | of neighbors    | : 1              |     |     |
| IP Address   |                 | : 169.254.125.33 |     |     |
| Interface    |                 | : vni1000        |     |     |
| Up Time      | (HH:MM:SS)      | : 00:44:35       |     |     |
| Expire       | Time (HH:MM:SS) | : 00:03:25       |     |     |
| DR Priority  |                 | : 1              |     |     |
| Hold Time    | (HH:MM:SS)      | : 00:03:30       |     |     |
IndeploymentswithanIPv4overlay,multicastrouteentrieswithanincomingL3VNIinterfacewillhave
aneighboripaddressthatisanauto-generatedIPv4addressderivedfromtheunderlayV6tunnel
address,

Command context

Authority

8400

10000

IGMP/MLD
In a VXLAN deployment, enabling IGMP/MLD snooping prevents flooding of unknown multicast packets
to all the VTEPs in the L2VNI. AHQ from the querier is sent to all the VTEPs in the L2VNI along with the
regular ports in the associated VLAN. Additionally, IGMP/MLD Membership Reports from local hosts are
forwarded to all the VTEPs in the L2VNI.

In a situation where a VSX pair is acting like a VTEP, IGMP/MLD Membership Reports from remote VTEPS
are synchronized among the VSX peers. Both the VSX peers learn the join on the same VTEP. In order to
achieve this, redistribute local-svi or redistribute local-mac commands must be enabled under
the EVPN context. When running VSX on a pair of switches, only one device gets the packet on the VTEP.
IGMP/MLD snooping should be enabled on all devices in the fabric, otherwise all multicast traffic is
flooded.

If the VTEP does not have any IGMP/MLD configurations, VXLAN encapsulated packets are copied to the CPU

even though there is no IGMP/MLD configuration; the packets are forwarded by the software to the other ports.

In a VXLAN-enabled topology where there are tunnel endpoints configured on different platforms, the scale

supported are the lowest value among the different platforms. This is because the joins are synced across the

Tunnel Endpoints.

PIM-SM
In a VXLAN deployment, when PIM is configured on the tenant VRF, PIM is automatically enabled on the
L3VNI interface on that VRF. This interface is used to route multicast traffic across tunnels in a
symmetric IRB network. PIM neighbors are learned on this interface and the remote VTEP neighbors are
listed under this interface. Multicast traffic is forwarded through the same L3VNI interface to reach
other VTEP clients. PIM neighbors formed across L3VNI can be seen using show ip pim neighbor or
show ipv6 pim6 neighbor. For a L3VNI neighbor, the neighbor IP is the underlay VTEP IP in the
Default VRF and all tenant VRFs. show ip mroute or show ipv6 mroutedisplays the multicast routes
ingressing and egressing on L3VNI interfaces.

PIM uses the VXLAN source IP as the DR priority and it overrides the default DR priority. It is
recommended not to configure DR priorities. However, if you must configure the priority, the DR priority
should be unique in each of the leaf nodes. For a VSX VTEP, the VSX peers should have the same DR
priority.

AOS-CX 10.12 Multicast Guide | (6200, 6300, 6400, 8xxx, 9300, 10000 Switch Series)

748

n PIM-DM with VXLAN is not currently supported.

n PIM-SM VxLAN with the MSDP, Anycast RP is not supported.

n Non-Anycast RP can be configured on extended L3-Router or on VxLAN VTEPs including VSX devices.

n MTrace in a PIM-SM VxLAN deployment is not currently supported.

n Multicast over static VXLAN tunnels is currently not supported.

n PIM active-active is not applicable for Multicast over VXLAN.

For IPv6, PIM auto-generates overlay IPv6 address from the underlay IPv4 tunnel source IP address. This
Auto-generated IP is used to form neighborship between L3VNI interfaces. Similarly, show commands
for mroute displays the multicast routes ingressing/egressing on L3VNI interfaces. The auto generated
IP is a link local IP. For example, if the tunnel source IP address is configured as 10.122.189.11, then the
auto-generated overlay IPv6 address would be fe80::10:122:189:11.

PIM-SSM
PIM-SSM over VXLAN supports the groups within SSM range addresses.The SSM range addresses can be
a default SSM group address range or can be defined using SSM range ACL.

PIM-SSM supports the legacy IGMPv1/IGMPv2/MLD1 clients using SSM-Map. PIM-SSM over VxLAN is
supported for IPv4 group address ranges.

PIM-SSM with Multifabric is supported for IPv4 address ranges. PIM-SSM with Multifabric supports the
legacy clients (i.e IGMPv1/IGMPv2/MLDv1) using SSM-Map. Any changes in PIM-SSM configurations clear
all internal states and Mroutes associated with L3VNI interface. This results in a short period of traffic
loss until PIM rebuilds the states.

Refer to Protocol Independent Multicast - Source-Specific Multicast for more information and related
commands.

Recommendations for PIM-SSM over VXLAN

Enable the PIM-SSM on the PIM-DR devices and client connected devices.

Configure PIM-SSM Map on PIM-DR, client connected VTEPs, or all devices where L2 VNI is extended.

Limitations

PIM-SSM is not supported with IPv6 underlay for PIM-SSM over VXLAN.

PIM-SSM is not supported with IPv6 mutlicast address ranges on Overlay.

Mroute output for SSM over VXLAN

All multicast route entries that carry PIM-SSM in the Multicast Routing Protocol field rerpresent an SSM
mroute.

This example shows an SSM mroute with incoming interface as L3VNI:

leaf1# show ip mroute 232.0.0.1 100.1.1.10 vrf red

Debugging and troubleshooting
The recommended multicast VXLAN troubleshooting flow for overlay IPv4 and IPv6 is provided in Figure
1, Recommended multicast VXLAN troubleshooting flow. Additional tips and guidance for each step are
provided below.

In cases of IPv6 overlay, use the IPv6 version of the commands. Examples in this section show the IPv4 version of

commands.

Figure 1 Recommended multicast VXLAN troubleshooting flow

Single fabric Multicast VXLAN (V4 and V6) | 771

The recommended IPv4 multicast VXLAN troubleshooting procedure includes the following sequence of
steps:

Step 1: Check underlay network reachability and VXLAN tunnels between VTEPs.

a. Ensure tunnel source/destination loopback IPs are correctly advertised in the underlay network.

b. Use extended pings on VTEPs to confirm network connectivity between tunnel source/destination

loopback IPs.

c. Fix underlay connectivity issues if discovered.

d.

If there are no underlay network issues, validate VXLAN tunnels, ensure they are operational.

AOS-CX 10.12 Multicast Guide | (6200, 6300, 6400, 8xxx, 9300, 10000 Switch Series)

772

VTEP1#
|     |        | sh int | vx vtep     |               |             |          |
| --- | ------ | ------ | ----------- | ------------- | ----------- | -------- |
|     | Source |        | Destination | Origin Status | VNI Routing | VLAN VRF |
------------- ---------------- -------- --------------- --------- --------- ----- ---
|     | 192.168.2.6 |     | 192.168.2.5 | evpn operational | 100001 enabled | --  |
| --- | ----------- | --- | ----------- | ---------------- | -------------- | --- |
VRF1
192.168.2.6 192.168.2.5 evpn operational 100111 disabled 111 --
|     | 192.168.2.6 |     | 192.168.11.3 | evpn operational | 100001 enabled | --  |
| --- | ----------- | --- | ------------ | ---------------- | -------------- | --- |
VRF1
|     | 192.168.2.6 |     | 192.168.11.3 | evpn operational | 100002 enabled | --  |
| --- | ----------- | --- | ------------ | ---------------- | -------------- | --- |
VRF2
e. IfEVPNtunnelisdown,ensurecorrectEVPNconfigsareused.
| Step | 2: Validate | multicast | specific | configurations. |     |     |
| ---- | ----------- | --------- | -------- | --------------- | --- | --- |
a. InadistributedL3gatewaydeployment,virtual-macconfigurationisneededonalltheVTEPs.
Makesurevirtual-macisconfiguredanditisuniqueperVTEP,aVSXVTEPpeerwouldshare1
virtual-mac:
|     | VTEP1#      | show | run | i virtual-mac |     |     |     |
| --- | ----------- | ---- | ------------------- | --- | --- | --- |
|     | virtual-mac |      | 00:00:22:00:00:21   |     |     |     |
b. OnVSXVTEPs,ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighborisrequiredonallSVIswherePIMis
enabled.
|     | interface | vlan40 |     |     |     |     |
| --- | --------- | ------ | --- | --- | --- | --- |
no shutdown
|     | vrf | attach     | red                  |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | ---------- | -------------------- | --- | --- | --- |
|     | ip  | address    | 40.40.40.1/24        |     |     |     |
|     | ip  | pim-sparse | enable               |     |     |     |
|     | ip  | pim-sparse | vsx-virtual-neighbor |     |     |     |
c. IfIGMPorMLDisenabledonanyVLAN,theredistribute local-sviorredistribute local-
maccommandmustbeenabledundertheevpncontext.Thisisneededforproperquerier
informationpropagation.
d. Validateunicastreachabilitytothemulticastsources/DR/RP/BSRaddressesacrossalltheVTEPs.
|      | Useshow     | ip routecommandtovalidatetheroutes.Forexample: |               |         |               |            |
| ---- | ----------- | ---------------------------------------------- | ------------- | ------- | ------------- | ---------- |
|      | VTEP1# show | ip route                                       | 80.1.1.1      | vrf red |               |            |
|      | VRF: red    |                                                |               |         |               |            |
|      | Prefix      |                                                | : 80.1.1.0/24 |         | VRF(egress)   | : -        |

FAQ
SinglefabricMulticastVXLAN(V4andV6)|781

1. Can I enable multicast on both the overlay (leaf/access VTEP) and underlay
(core/spine non-VTEP)?

No, it should not be done and is not recommended. The solution will not work if PIM or IGMP is enabled
on the core/spine switches connected to VTEPs.

2. Does the solution require EVPN control plane?

Yes, the solution requires EVPN based control plane both for L2 and L3 multicast. Remote VTEPs are
learned via EVPN type 3. In addition, remote hosts and routes are learned via EVPN type 2 and EVPN
type 5. IGMP and PIM protocols rely on this information to forward packets.

3. Is PIM-SM used in underlay for BUM traffic replication?

No, the solution does not use PIM-SM at underlay to replicate BUM traffic. The solution uses Head End
Replication to send multicast traffic to remote VTEPs, this means that each broadcast/multicast is
encapsulated and replicated to all VTEPs from the source VTEP. The list of VTEPs to be replicated can be
learned via EVPN type 3 or through multicast protocols running in the overlay.

4. Are there are any platform differences to be aware of?

n For the VSX VTEPs on the Aruba 8325, 8400, 9300, and 10000 Switch Series, when a packet is routed
from a SVI/L3VNI to a L3VNI/L2VNI, only the primary VSX VTEP forwards. This prevents duplication of
packets.

n For the VSX VTEPs on the Aruba 6400, 8100, and 8360 Switch Series , whichever device gets data
directly (not via ISL) will forward. This is done because these platforms have a predicate rule
programmed that any packet over ISL will not be sent over tunnel. This means there will never be a
case when packets go over the VSX ISL and into a VXLAN tunnel.

5. Is the Active-Active command required on VSX VTEPs?

The Active-Active command is optional for VSX VTEPs. This is because VSX peers act as logical VTEPs and
work in Active-Active mode natively. There is no need to explicitly enable Active-Active on VSX VTEPS.

6. Why is the VSX virtual neighbor command required?

n The VSX virtual neighbor command needs to be enabled on all PIM enabled SVI interfaces with VSX-

LAGs and L2 VNI mapped interfaces, the command once enabled does multiple things.

n If the VLAN is the first hop connected router, then both the VSX VTEPs will become DR and will

forward PIM joins for every IGMP join that received.

n If the VLAN is the not the first-hop connected router, then both the VSX VTEPs will ignore the prime

neighbor check and will forward PIM joins upstream.

7. Does PIM need to be enabled on the L3 VNI?

No, PIM-SM does not need to be enabled on the L3 VNI. It will be automatically enabled when PIM-SM is
enabled on the tenant VRF. A VTEP forms PIM neighbors with the other remote VTEPs over L3VNI. AOS-
CX forms automatic PIM-SM neighbors with peer VTEPs over L3VNI as soon as PIM-SM is enabled on the
tenant VRF.

8. Does the solution work for all types of underlay interfaces?

The solution work with all types of underlay interfaces that are currently supported in AOS-CX.

9. If I change the DR priority of my DR on an SVI with multicast VXLAN, will it work?

AOS-CX 10.12 Multicast Guide | (6200, 6300, 6400, 8xxx, 9300, 10000 Switch Series)

782

In a logical VSX VTEP, DR priority is automatically derived from the VTEP address. This ensures that the
VSX VTEPs have identical priorities with both of them functioning as DRs or as non DRs. In case DR
priority is changed, one of the VSX peer will start behaving differently from the other. To conclude, do
not change DR priority on SVIs of VSX VTEPs.

10. In a VSX VTEP, are VSX keepalive links mandatory for multicast VXLAN?

The VSX keepalive is not mandatory as private IP is used between VSX peers is used to sync joins
received over L3 VNIs

11. Why do I require an additional L3 link between VSX border VTEPs when there is
ROP/P2P SVI extension?

The additional connectivity is required from the VSX border VTEP to provide an additional link in case
the main uplink connectivity from the primary VSX peer goes down. In general the rule that is
programmed is that only VSX primary VTEP will forward. If uplink connectivity from the primary VTEP
goes down then PIM joins from primary are sent to the secondary and the traffic is pulled from
secondary towards the primary.

12. How do I identify an IPv6 PIM neighbor learned on a L3 VNI interface?

PIM auto-generates an overlay IPv6 address from the underlay IPv4 tunnel source IP address. This Auto-
generated IP is used to form neighborship between L3VNI interfaces. The auto generated IP is a link
local IP. For instance, if the VXLAN tunnel source IP is configured as 10.122.189.11, then the auto-
generatedIPv6 overlay address is fe80::10:122:189:11. In other words, fe80::10:122:189:11 represents a
VTEP neighbor with a VXLAN tunnel source IP of 10.122.189.11.

n AOS-CX VXLAN EVPN Symmetric IRB Distributed L3 Gateways

In some deployments, splitting a site into multiple fabrics to support the required VTEP scale is
necessary.

Figure 3, Multiple fabrics per site shows an example of multiple fabrics per site, where one fabric per
site is configured to a border leader VTEP. This helps to minimize full mesh eBGP peering to other sites.
Border VTEPs in other fabrics within a site only eBGP peer to the border leader VTEP.

Figure 3 Multiple fabrics per site

Multi-fabric multicast VXLAN | 785

With eBGP EVPN peering established, full multi-fabric EVPN VXLAN tunnels are dynamically formed, as
shown in Figure 4, Dynamically formed full miulti-fabric EVPN VXLAN tunnels.

Figure 4 Dynamically formed full miulti-fabric EVPN VXLAN tunnels

AOS-CX 10.12 Multicast Guide | (6200, 6300, 6400, 8xxx, 9300, 10000 Switch Series)

786

Protocol and feature details
A campus/ DC network can be divided into multiple fabrics for the following scenarios:

Campuses are geographically distributed

VTEPs may scale poorly after a certain limit

Refer to Protocol and feature details for single fabric multicast VXLAN for more information for protocol
and feature details for Multi-fabric Multicast VXLAN.

RP Placement

RPs do not need to be present at every site. In multi-fabric topology, RP can be placed at one site
centrally or can be configured at multiple site to mitigate site failures. A Border router or Leaf router can
be configured as RP.

Both dynamic and static RPs are supported. The general recommendation is to place the RP at the site
where the majority of multicast sources are present. An RP which is outside the fabric is also supported,
provided that the unicast and multicast reachability is there to the external RP. Anycast RP and MSDP
are not supported on the fabric.

Multi-fabric multicast VXLAN | 787

788
| AOS-CX10.12MulticastGuide| | (6200,6300,6400,8xxx,9300,10000SwitchSeries) |     |     |     |
| -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | --- | --- | --- |

n L2 VSX LAG: Upstream or Downstream routers are connected via L2 VSX LAG links.

n L3 VSX LAG: Upstream or Downstream routers are connected via L3 VSX LAG links.

n ROP extension: L3 extension for Sources/Clients via Route Only Ports is supported.

n Point to Point SVI extension: L3 extension for Sources/Clients via Point to Point SVIs is supported.

If the source is connected via Route only Port or via Point to Point SVI, it is recommended to have an
additional L3 Link per VRF between the VSX devices for upstream redundancy. If the L3 link is a SVI, it is
recommended to not add ISL port in that VLAN.

Packet forwarding rules at the border router

PIM Hellos are periodically sent to both intra-fabric and inter-fabric VTEP tunnels when the Hello timer
expires on the L3VNI interface. Because the border routers are connected to both inter and intra
tunnels, Hellos from the local VTEPs as well from the remote border VTEPs are received. The border
VTEP learns both intra-fabric VTEP and the remote border leaf VTEP under the L3VNI interfece.

The border VTEP does not re-forward the PIM hellos from one fabric to another.

Multicast Routing Across Fabrics
The typical deployment for multi-fabric will have receiver and source distributed at different sites (see
Figure 1, Receiver and source at different sites).

In Figure 1, Receiver and source at different sites, a receiver request from site B for multicast group is
processed by the VTEP B router where the receiver is present. RP is configured on the border router at
site A. VTEP B determines that the Border VTEP is the Nexthop to reach the RP. Site A's border VTEP,
which is the RP, processes the PIM join and then initiates S, G PIM join towards Site A's VTEP router,
where the source is connected. Traffic then flows from Site A's border VTEP to Site A's border VTEP, then
from there to Site B's Border VTEP, then finally towards Site B's border VTEP to the client.

All four VTEPs in this path install the Mroutes. The border VTEPs on both sites route traffic from one to
tunnel to another. The VTEP A border VTEPs from the source interface to the tunnel and the VTEP B
border VTEPs from the tunnel to the receiver's interface.

At the Border VTEP, the list of outgoing VTEPs are updated in the Intra-Inter Forwarded VTEP(s) section
of the Mroute table. This field contains the combined set of inter and intra tunnels where the packet
needs to be forwarded. If the packet is ingressing from the intra-fabric tunnel, it is forwarded only to the
list of inter-fabric tunnels listed in this field. If the packet is ingressing from the inter-fabric tunnel, it is
forwarded only to the list of intra-fabric tunnels listed in this field.

Figure 1 Receiver and source at different sites

Multi-fabric multicast VXLAN | 789

PIM-SSM
PIM-SSM over multi-fabric is supported and inherits the functionality of PIM-SM over multi-fabric. The
PIM-SSM over multi-fabric supports only IPv4 address ranges. PIM-SSM with Multifabric supports the
legacy clients (IGMPv1/IGMPv2) using SSM-Map.

Considerations and Best Practices
Refer to Considerations and best practices for Single fabric Multicast VXLAN for more information for
protocol and feature details.

AOS-CX suports MCLAG, ROP, and P2P SVI extensions between the Border VTEP and External
router (non-VXLAN). The MCLAG extension is preferred.

RP and Source must be reachable from other fabrics.

Use Cases
The use cases provide examples of multi-fabric multicast VXLAN.

distance 250

set tag 120

route-map tag_ospf_bgp deny seq 10

match tag 110

route-map tag_ospf_bgp permit seq 30
!

router-id 111.12.1.3
distance 250
redistribute bgp
redistribute connected
redistribute local loopback
area 0.0.0.0

router ospf 1 vrf vrf1

router-id 111.12.1.5
distance 250
redistribute bgp route-map tag_bgp_ospf
redistribute connected
area 0.0.0.0

router ospf 1 vrf vrf2

router-id 111.12.2.5
distance 250
redistribute bgp route-map tag_bgp_ospf
redistribute connected
area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 65535

bgp router-id 111.12.1.3
neighbor 2.2.2.2 remote-as 65535
neighbor 2.2.2.2 update-source loopback 1
neighbor 40.1.3.2 remote-as 65536
neighbor 40.1.4.2 remote-as 65536
neighbor 192.168.1.5 remote-as 65535
neighbor 192.168.1.5 update-source loopback 1

neighbor 196.170.1.255 ebgp-multihop 5
neighbor 196.170.1.255 update-source loopback 113
address-family ipv4 unicast

neighbor 40.1.3.2 activate
neighbor 40.1.4.2 activate
redistribute connected
redistribute local loopback

exit-address-family

neighbor 2.2.2.2 send-community extended

neighbor 192.168.1.5 send-community extended
neighbor 192.168.1.5 activate

neighbor 196.170.1.255 activate

conf t
!export-password: default
hostname site1-Access-vtep-1
vrf vrf1
rd 31:11
| route-target | export 1:100 | evpn |
| ------------ | ------------ | ---- |
| route-target | import 1:100 | evpn |
vrf vrf2
rd 32:11
| route-target | export 2:100 | evpn |
| ------------ | ------------ | ---- |
| route-target | import 2:100 | evpn |
cli-session
| timeout 0 |     |     |
| --------- | --- | --- |
!
!
!
!
!
!
ssh server vrf default
ssh server vrf mgmt
| vsf secondary-member | 2   |     |
| -------------------- | --- | --- |
vsf member 1
type r8s90a
link 1 1/1/28
vlan 1,4
vlan 101
| ip igmp snooping | enable |     |
| ---------------- | ------ | --- |
vlan 102
| ip igmp snooping | enable |     |
| ---------------- | ------ | --- |
virtual-mac 00:01:20:11:56:20
evpn
arp-suppression
nd-suppression
| redistribute | local-mac |     |
| ------------ | --------- | --- |
| redistribute | local-svi |     |
vlan 101
rd auto
| route-target | export     | 101:141 |
| ------------ | ---------- | ------- |
| route-target | import     | 101:141 |
| redistribute | host-route |         |
Multi-fabricmulticastVXLAN|841

vlan 102

rd auto
route-target export 102:142
route-target import 102:142
redistribute host-route

spanning-tree
interface mgmt

no shutdown
ip dhcp

interface lag 36
shutdown
no routing
vlan access 1

interface lag 37

no shutdown
no routing
vlan trunk native 1
vlan trunk allowed 4
lacp mode active
lacp rate fast

interface 1/1/1

description port_ixia
no shutdown
no routing
vlan trunk native 1
vlan trunk allowed 101-102

interface 1/1/49

description port_Site-1-Distro-vsx-primary
no shutdown
lag 37

interface 1/1/50

description port_Site-1-Distro-vsx-secondary
no shutdown
lag 37

interface loopback 1

ip address 192.168.1.5/32
ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface loopback 15

ip address 3.3.3.50/32
ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface vlan 1

ip dhcp

interface vlan 4

ip address 40.1.1.4/29
l3-counters
ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface vlan 101

vrf attach vrf1
ip address 11.1.1.10/24
active-gateway ip mac 00:00:11:16:16:00
active-gateway ip 11.1.1.10
ip igmp enable
ip pim-sparse enable

interface vlan 102

vrf attach vrf2
ip address 11.1.2.10/24
active-gateway ip mac 00:00:11:16:16:00
active-gateway ip 11.1.2.10
ip igmp enable
ip pim-sparse enable

interface vxlan 1

source ip 3.3.3.50

!
!
!
!
ssh server vrf default
ssh server vrf mgmt
vlan 1
vlan 101

ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 111

ip igmp snooping enable

virtual-mac 00:01:20:11:56:25
evpn

arp-suppression
nd-suppression
redistribute local-mac
redistribute local-svi
vlan 101

rd auto
route-target export 101:101
route-target import 101:101
redistribute host-route

vlan 111

rd auto
route-target export 111:111
route-target import 111:111
redistribute host-route

interface 1/1/25

description port_Site-1-Border-vtep-Primary
no shutdown
ip address 30.1.2.0/31
ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0
ip ospf network point-to-point

interface 1/1/26

description port_Site-1-Border-vtep-Secondary
no shutdown
ip address 31.1.2.0/31
ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0
ip ospf network point-to-point

interface 1/1/27

description port_ixia
no shutdown
no routing
vlan trunk native 1
vlan trunk allowed 101,111

interface loopback 1

ip address 2.2.2.2/32
ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface vxlan 1

source ip 2.2.2.2
no shutdown
vni 2000

vni 2010

vlan 101

vlan 111

!
!
!
router ospf 1

router-id 111.14.1.3

neighbor 40.1.1.1 remote-as 65535
neighbor 40.1.3.1 remote-as 65535
neighbor 45.1.1.2 remote-as 65536
neighbor 85.85.85.85 remote-as 65536
neighbor 85.85.85.85 update-source loopback 114
neighbor 193.170.1.255 remote-as 65535
neighbor 193.170.1.255 ebgp-multihop 5
neighbor 193.170.1.255 update-source loopback 113
neighbor 194.170.1.255 remote-as 65535
neighbor 194.170.1.255 ebgp-multihop 5
neighbor 194.170.1.255 update-source loopback 113
address-family ipv4 unicast

neighbor 40.1.1.1 activate
neighbor 40.1.3.1 activate
neighbor 45.1.1.2 activate
redistribute connected
redistribute local loopback

exit-address-family
address-family l2vpn evpn

neighbor 4.4.4.4 next-hop-self
neighbor 4.4.4.4 route-reflector-client
neighbor 4.4.4.4 send-community extended
neighbor 4.4.4.4 activate
neighbor 85.85.85.85 next-hop-self
neighbor 85.85.85.85 route-reflector-client
neighbor 85.85.85.85 send-community extended
neighbor 85.85.85.85 activate
neighbor 193.170.1.255 send-community extended
neighbor 193.170.1.255 activate
neighbor 194.170.1.255 send-community extended
neighbor 194.170.1.255 activate

!

!

exit-address-family

vrf vrf1

address-family ipv4 unicast

redistribute connected
redistribute local loopback
redistribute ospf route-map tag_ospf_bgp

exit-address-family

vrf vrf2

address-family ipv4 unicast

redistribute connected
redistribute local loopback
redistribute ospf route-map tag_ospf_bgp

exit-address-family

!
router pim vrf vrf1

enable
pim-ssm

router pim vrf vrf2

ip prefix-list test_1 seq 30 deny 195.170.1.255/32

match tag 110

match tag 120

route-map tag_ospf_bgp permit seq 30
route-map test_1 permit seq 10

match ip address prefix-list test_1

!
router ospf 1

router-id 111.12.1.3
distance 250
redistribute connected
redistribute local loopback
redistribute bgp route-map test_1
area 0.0.0.0

distance 250

active-gateway ip mac 00:00:11:16:16:00
active-gateway ip 15.3.1.10
ip igmp enable
ip pim-sparse enable
ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor

interface vlan 1702

vrf attach vrf2
ip address 15.3.2.10/24
active-gateway ip mac 00:00:11:16:16:00
active-gateway ip 15.3.2.10
ip igmp enable
ip pim-sparse enable
ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor

interface vxlan 1

source ip 8.8.8.8
no shutdown
vni 2100

vlan 1501

vni 2101

vni 2300

vni 2301

vni 9002

vlan 1502

vlan 1701

vlan 1702

vrf vrf2
routing

vni 16777214

vrf vrf1
routing

vsx

system-mac 00:01:70:41:56:40
inter-switch-link lag 120

Multi-fabric multicast VXLAN | 1067

role primary
keepalive peer 47.1.1.2 source 47.1.1.1

ip prefix-list test_1 seq 20 deny 193.170.1.255/32
ip prefix-list test_1 seq 30 deny 194.170.1.255/32
ip prefix-list test_1 seq 40 deny 195.170.1.255/32
ip prefix-list test_1 seq 50 deny 196.170.1.255/32
ip prefix-list test_1 seq 60 permit 4.4.4.50/32
ip prefix-list test_1 seq 70 permit 80.80.80.80/32
!
!
!
!
route-map test_1 permit seq 10

match ip address prefix-list test_1

!
router ospf 1

router-id 113.11.1.3
distance 250
redistribute connected
redistribute local loopback
redistribute bgp route-map test_1
area 0.0.0.0

router ospf 1 vrf vrf1

router-id 113.11.1.5
distance 250
redistribute connected
redistribute local loopback
area 0.0.0.0

router ospf 1 vrf vrf2

router-id 113.11.2.5
distance 250
redistribute connected
redistribute local loopback
area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 65537

bgp router-id 113.11.1.3
neighbor 13.13.13.13 remote-as 65537
neighbor 13.13.13.13 update-source loopback 113
neighbor 14.14.14.14 remote-as 65537
neighbor 14.14.14.14 update-source loopback 113
neighbor 48.1.1.2 remote-as 65535
neighbor 48.1.2.2 remote-as 65535
neighbor 193.170.1.255 remote-as 65535
neighbor 193.170.1.255 ebgp-multihop 5
neighbor 193.170.1.255 update-source loopback 113
neighbor 194.170.1.255 remote-as 65535
neighbor 194.170.1.255 ebgp-multihop 5
neighbor 194.170.1.255 update-source loopback 113
neighbor 195.170.1.255 remote-as 65536
neighbor 195.170.1.255 ebgp-multihop 5
neighbor 195.170.1.255 update-source loopback 113
neighbor 196.170.1.255 remote-as 65536
neighbor 196.170.1.255 ebgp-multihop 5
neighbor 196.170.1.255 update-source loopback 113
address-family ipv4 unicast

neighbor 48.1.1.2 activate
neighbor 48.1.2.2 activate
redistribute connected
redistribute local loopback

exit-address-family
address-family l2vpn evpn

neighbor 13.13.13.13 next-hop-self

| Multicast |     | VXLAN |     | Multi-fabric |     | commands |

show ip mroute <GROUP ADDRESS> <SOURCE ADDRESS> [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>] detail
Description
Showsmulticastroutinginformationfromaborderrouterwheretrafficfromalocalsiteisforwardedto
aremotesite.Optionally,youcanshowspecificinformationbyVRF.Ifnooptionsarespecified,itshows
informationforthedefaultVRF.
| Parameter      |     |     |     |     | Description                                  |     |
| -------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | -------------------------------------------- | --- |
| all-vrfs       |     |     |     |     | ShowsallPIM neighborsinformation.            |     |
| vrf <VRF-NAME> |     |     |     |     | ShowsPIM neighborinformationforaspecificVRF. |     |
Usage
TheIntra-InterForwardedVTEP(s)tunnelfielddenoteswhichtunnelthetrafficisroutedto.Thisincludes
theintraorinterVTEPsbasedonwherethejoinsareseen.Ifthereismorethanonesitewherethe
trafficneedstobeforwarded,thecorrespondingsite'sVTEPsarelisted.TheIntra-InterForwardedVTEP
(s)fieldisonlyvisibleattheBorderrouter.
1080

TherearenooutgoinginterfaceslistedintheseMroutesastheyareroutingwithingthesameL3VNI
logicalinterface.IfthereareanylocalreceiversattheborderroutersthatareextendedviaL2/L3
extensions,thenthesameMrouteisupdatedwiththeoutgoinglist.

route(show ip route)andPIMRPF (show ip pim rpf)showcommandscanbeusedtoverifythe

VTEP.RPisconfiguredatSite2-borderVTEP.WhentheSourcestartsthetraffic,itregisterswithRP

interfacetoforwardthetraffictoPrimaryVSX.Thus,thesamemrouteentryhasdata-path-auto-

| Frequently      |            | Asked            | Questions |              |            |        |
1. When does the ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include command need to be enabled?
ThiscommandisapplicableonlyinthecaseofVSXVTEP,wherePrimaryorSecondaryhasanL3
connection(P2PorROP)tothenon-VXLANroutednetwork.
2. Is orphan port on primary and secondary VSX VTEP supported?
Yes,orphanportsaresupportedonlyintheformofROPandP2Pports.
Additionally,datapath-auto-includeneedstobeprovisionedbetweentheVSXpeersinthosescenarios.
| 3. Which | command | is specific |     | to Border | VTEP? |     |
| -------- | ------- | ----------- | --- | --------- | ----- | --- |
Thedyn-vxlan-tunnel-bridging-mode ibgp-ebgpcommandisspecifictotheborderVTEP.Itmustbe
configuredontheborderroutertoallowmulticastroutingbetweeniBGPandeBGP.Unlikeforunicast
1094
| AOS-CX10.12MulticastGuide| |     | (6200,6300,6400,8xxx,9300,10000SwitchSeries) |     |     |     |     |
| -------------------------- | --- | -------------------------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

routing, this command must be enabled regardless of whether the VLAN is stretched across fabrics,
ensuring proper functioning of multicast on the border router.

4. Why are redistribute-local-mac or redistribute-local-svi commands required for
IGMP/MLD?

IGMP/MLD running in a VSX VTEP is interested in the local MAC address of remote VTEPs for proper
synchronization of IGMP/MLD states between them. These commands are needed when at least one
VSX VTEP is present in the fabric and must be configured on all VTEPs (standalone and VSX VTEPs).
These commands are applicable for both single and multi-fabric topology.

Multi-fabric multicast VXLAN | 1095

Multicastroutingandbridging|1103

Chapter 14

Static routes

Static routes

Static multicast routing is not supported on the 9300 switch series.

Route summarization is not supported on the 8400 and 9300 switch series.

Static multicast routes can be configured by specifying the incoming interface, source, group, and
outgoing interfaces. Multicast static routes can be used to configure a static multicast flow and program
it directly into the IP multicast forwarding tables instead of running dynamic multicast protocols. Unlike
PIM, this feature does not depend on Unicast Routing Protocols to build the Multicast tree. Instead, it
works as a standalone Multicast Route provisioning feature that can interoperate with the dynamic
Multicast protocols like PIM-SM and L2 protocols like IGMP.

Protocol and Feature Details
Static multicast routing avoids processing overhead on routers by providing the flexibility to program a
specific path for mutlicast traffic from the source to the client without having to rely on underlying
protocols. Static multicast routes require users to explicitly specify the incoming interface, source
address, group address, and the set of downstream interfaces where the traffic needs to be replicated.

A static multicast route for both IPv4 and IPv6 protocol stacks can be programmed in the following
configurations:

n On a specified source. Traffic is forwarded from a specific source or a summarized entry for a specific

multicast group address from any source.

n No specified outgoing interface. This is used if there is a need to drop traffic without relaying traffic to

any of the downstream interfaces or to bridge traffic on the upstream interface.

A static multicast route is considered active as far as the incoming interface and the port(s) on which the
incoming traffic is replicated is operational. The interfaces that are down are not included in the
forwarding port list of the multicast entry programmed.

When a static multicast route is configured, it stays until the user removes it. It is recommended to use
static multicast routes for persistent traffic flows or in scenarios where resource usage needs to be
optimized by summarizing the static multicast route. Static multicast route does not timeout based on
traffic inactivity and the user should unconfigure the static multicast route configured promptly to avoid
stale routes or unwanted traffic misdirection.

To match on any source for a group, any can be specified, instead of a IP address for the source. Multiple
multicast routes having the same portset (incoming interface, group, outgoing interfaces and l2) can be
summarized to a single route to avoid multiple routes and efficiently utilize the hardware resources.
Summarization is enabled by default and is applied only on /32 or 0.0.0.0/0 multicast routes.

When a multicast static route is configured on the source connected router, PIM registration process for
that route will be suppressed and PIM joins will be dropped.

AOS-CX 10.12 Multicast Guide | (6200, 6300, 6400, 8xxx, 9300, 10000 Switch Series)

1104

When a multicast static route is configured on the RP router, it sends a PIM register stop message if it
receives a PIM register message and clears pending registrations for that source and group. This feature
can be used for handling SSDP traffic in a network.

Summarized static routes can be used in the end-to-end path between multicast source and clients in
such cases. It is highly recommended to not use static multicast routes to replace PIM in existing
deployments for other group addresses and usecases.

The following is an example of a typical static multicast route:

[Incoming interface, Source, Group] > [Set of downstream interfaces]

In this example, the Incoming interface is the L3 interface from which the traffic is received on the
router, Source is the source IP address of the stream device, and Group is the multicast IP address of the
group for which the traffic is streamed. The downstream interfaces are the L3 interfaces on which PIM
joins are received or to which the receivers are connected.

Figure 1 Configuring summarized static multicast route to prevent high resource utilization

Deployments

Static Multicast Routes can be used in static-only mode, hybrid mode with PIM, and Redistribution into
PIM.

Static-only mode

Only multicast static routes are used to route multicast packets between Source and Client designated
router. All the devices are configured with static multicast routes for all the multicast flows, and PIM
does not need not be enabled on the network. IGMP/MLD is enabled on this network to learn joins from
clients. This may not be deployed in common and only small-sized networks where the multicast source
and clients are only a few hops apart should be configured to run in this mode. Dynamic multicast
routing protocols like PIM are not used in this network for multicast routing. IGMP/MLD is enabled on
this network to learn joins from clients.

Figure 2 Static-only mode

Static routes | 1105

Hybrid mode with PIM

In this mode, multicast static routes are configured on all devices between Source and Client designated
routers to route multicast packets for some specific flows. Dynamic multicast routing protocols, like PIM,
are also enabled in these devices to route multicast traffic for other flows. The multicast routers have
both static multicast routes and PIM mroutes programmed in the multicast forwarding table, but the
entries are mutually exclusive. IGMP/MLD is enabled on this network to learn joins from clients. Only the
flows that have scope of summarization or the flows for which the multicast network seldom changes
can be picked to be configured as static multicast routes in all the devices along the path.

In hybrid networks, the routers including RP and all routers towards Source from RP should be part of
the PIM enabled network. Static multicast routing can be enabled on the routers connected towards
Client side. If deployed in hybrid mode, all the border routers between Static and PIM networks operate
in hybrid mode. This mode works with PIM-SM, PIM-DM and PIM-SSM.

Figure 3 Hybrid mode with PIM

AOS-CX 10.12 Multicast Guide | (6200, 6300, 6400, 8xxx, 9300, 10000 Switch Series)

1106

Redistribution mode into PIM

In this mode, a combination of PIM and multicast static routes are used in the network to route
multicast packets from Source to clients that are interested in multicast traffic. In this mode, the routers,
including RP and all routers towards Source from RP, should be part of the PIM enabled network. Static
multicast routing is enabled on the routers connected towards Client side with a redistributing router
that is enabled for static multicast route stitching the PIM and Static multicast route domains.
IGMP/MLD is enabled on this network to learn joins from clients. If deployed in redistribution mode, all
the border routers between Static and PIM networks should operate in redistribution mode and care
should be taken while configuring to avoid duplicate packets caused by multiple routers redistributing
PIM joins for the same multicast flow. This mode is recommended in cases where the device is
connected towards the multicast receivers that are affected by join/leave latency issues due to high
multicast route scale.

Figure 4 Redistribution mode into PIM

Scenario 2: Traffic is duplicated due to {S, G} and {*, G} from different upstreams

In this scenario, a summarized static multicast route {*, G} is configured on Router1 and {S, G} is
configured on Router2 where the downstream of these routers intersects at a certain point. Traffic
coming on both Router1 and Router2 forwards traffic to the downstream and the device where these
traffic intersects sends duplicate traffic downstream. Overlapping multicast static routes should not be
configured in these cases to avoid duplicate traffic.

Figure 2 Traffic is duplicated due to {S, G} and {*, G} from different upstreams

Static routes | 1109

PIM + Static Scenarios

This section explains the multicast traffic flow and protocol behavior for various combinations of PIM
and Static multicast route enabled in a network. These scenarios are not recommended.

The following notations are used in this section:

n Static {*, G}: Summarized static route “ip multicast-static-route <upstream_interface> any <group_ip

== G> <downstream_interface>” configured on the device.

n Static {S, G}: Summarized static route “ip multicast-static-route <upstream_interface> <source_ip ==

S> <group_ip == G> <downstream_interface>” configured on the device.

n PIM: PIM is enabled on all interfaces of the device.

n PIM-SSM: PIM-SSM is enabled for the group G.

n Redistribute: Static multicast routes are redistributed into PIM domain on this device.

n Forward: Traffic sent from the Source is received by the Client successfully.

n Drop: Traffic sent from the Source is not received by the Client.

n RPT: Root Path Tree.

n SPT: Source Path Tree.

Figure 3 Sample multicast network

VSX Scenarios

Static multicast route configuration on VSX based networks is supported. There are configuration
constraints related to static multicast routes on the primary and secondary devices in the VSX pair. On a
symmetric VSX network, traffic is always routed by the primary device on a mc-lag and the secondary
device programs bridge entries for multicast traffic. In cases where the the primary fails, the other
device in the VSX pair starts routing traffic on mc-lag.

The following notations are used in this section:

n Symmetric Upstream: Symmetric upstream indicates the upstream interface for multicast traffic is

the same on both primary and secondary devices for the VSX pair.

n Asymmetric Upstream (Primary): Asymmetric upstream indicates the upstream interface for
multicast traffic is not the same on both primary and secondary devices for the VSX pair. The
multicast source is connected on an upstream interface which is specific to VSX primary device.

n Asymmetric Upstream (Secondary): Asymmetric upstream indicates the upstream interface for
multicast traffic is not the same on both primary and secondary devices for the VSX pair. The
multicast source is connected on an upstream interface which is specific to VSX secondary device.

n Symmetric Downstream: Symmetric downstream indicates that the downstream interface for

multicast traffic is the same on both primary and secondary devices for the VSX pair. The multicast
receivers are reachable through the same interface from both VSX primary and secondary devices.

n Asymmetric Downstream: Asymmetric downstream indicates that the downstream interface on

which the multicast receivers are connected is specific to either VSX primary or secondary device.

n Route: Indicates configuration of static multicast route with an upstream interface and one of more

downstream interfaces.

n Bridge: Indicates configuration of static multicast route with only upstream interface so that traffic

matching this flow will be bridged only on the ports of upstream interface.

Table 3, VSX Scenarios describes various scenarios and the configuration principle of static multicast
route in VSX deployments.
