Static routes | 1133

ECMP scenarios

Static multicast routes are not recommended for use in deployments where ECMP towards
upstream/downstream is present, as they can cause duplicate traffic. It is not recommended to
configure static multicast routes on the networks where PIM solves duplicate packets with solutions
such as Designated Forwarder Election.

ECMP downstream

ECMP downstream occurs when the same multicast receivers are reachable from a router over multiple
paths. When a static multicast route is configured with multiple downstream interfaces on this device to
route traffic through these paths, duplicate packets are seen on the multicast receiver. Such networks
with strict requirement of backup paths should not be considered for static multicast routes.

Figure 28 ECMP downstream on R1 with static multicast routes

ECMP upstream with static multicast routes

Consider a network with end-to-end static multicast routes configured to forward multicast traffic for a
specific flow. If there are multiple equal cost paths to reach the multicast source on a router, configuring
multicast routes with both these upstreams can cause traffic to be received from both paths, resulting
in duplicate packets.

Figure 29 ECMP upstream on R4 with static multicast routes

AOS-CX 10.12 Multicast Guide | (6200, 6300, 6400, 8xxx, 9300, 10000 Switch Series)

1134

In Figure 29, ECMP upstream on R4 with static multicast routes R4 is the device for which there is ECMP
upstream. The multicast static route configuration on routers R1, R3, and R4 are marked as invalid to
showcase the duplicate traffic data path. Refer to ECMP downstream with summary route as backup for
more information on achieving redundancy.

ECMP upstream with PIM

Duplicate traffic may be seen when static multicast route is redistributed into the upstream PIM
domain. If multiple PIM routers in the ECMP path receive joins directly from the redistributing router on
the downstream, duplicate traffic is received by the multicast receivers.

Figure 30 ECMP upstream with redistribute static multicast routes

Static routes | 1135

ECMP downstream with summary route as backup

For a router with two ECMP paths to reach the multicast receiver(s) and if there is a unique source (and
no multiple sources) sending traffic to a specific group address, configuring an {S, G} route and a {*, G}
route for the same group proves redundancy in cases of local link failures. When such overlapping static
multicast routes are configured on a device, traffic is forwarded using the {S, G} entry based on the
longest prefix match on path1. If the interface configured as downstream for {S, G} goes down, {*, G}
route is used for traffic forwarding through the alternate path. Since this configuration cannot provide
redundancy in case of path failures and selects backup path only on local link failures, the application
scenarios are very limited.

Figure 31 ECMP downstream with summary route as backup

AOS-CX 10.12 Multicast Guide | (6200, 6300, 6400, 8xxx, 9300, 10000 Switch Series)

1136

Inter-operability
Multicast routing protocols like PIM and IGMP are responsible for construction of the multicast delivery
tree on overlay networks. These traditional multicast routing protocols achieve this by exchanging
mroutes in control packets along with configuring policies across all devices in the network. Static
multicast routes interoperate with PIM, IGMP, and other vendors.

Inter-operability with PIM

Arbitration with PIM

Arbitration with PIM is needed when a statically configured multicast route is also learnt by PIM. User
configured multicast routes take precedence over the PIM learnt routes. If a static multicast route is
configured for a flow and is active, PIM does not listen for any updates of this specific flow and does not
process the PIM control packets or corresponding data packets corresponding on the incoming
configured interface. If a summarized static mroute is configured, the multicast packets from any source
or matching PIM control packets for the con figured group address do not be processed by PIM. If PIM
has already learnt an mroute and a matching static multicast route is configured, the multicast route
learn by PIM is removed from the forwarding table and static multicast route is programmed.

Redistribution with PIM

Static multicast routes give the flexibility to the user to program a specific path for multicast traffic from
the source to the client without having to rely on the underlying protocols to build a multicast route.
They can be configured on all the routers in the path or on a specific section of routers. The remaining
section of routers can be configured to run the native PIM protocol. In Figure 1, Redistributing Static
Mroutes into PIM domain static multicast routes are configured on routers R4 and R5 whereas PIM
protocol builds the multicast routes on routers R1 and R2. R3 acts as a gateway where the static
multicast routes terminate and PIM initiates.

Figure 1 Redistributing Static Mroutes into PIM domain

Static routes | 1137

In Figure1,RedistributingStaticMroutesintoPIMdomain,onR3,astaticmrouteisprogrammedas
follows:
| Flow | Intercoming | interface | Outgoing Interface |
| ---- | ----------- | --------- | ------------------ |
| S,G  | Iface1      |           | Iface2             |

follows:
| Flow | Intercoming | interface | Outgoing Interface |
| ---- | ----------- | --------- | ------------------ |
| S,G  | Iface2      |           | Iface3             |

onR3,butnotonR4.ThesourceregisterstoR2(RP)throughthenativePIMpath.Asaresult,R1andR2

commandisconfiguredtoinform PIM aboutthestaticmulticastroute.ThisensuresthatPIMcreatesan
Upstream(S,G)stateandsendsanS,GjointoR2throughIface1.ThisjoinreachesR1,whichforwards
thesourcetraffictoR2.R2startsroutingthe(S,G)traffictoIface1.ThistrafficisroutedonR3,R4,and

| AOS-CX10.12MulticastGuide| | (6200,6300,6400,8xxx,9300,10000SwitchSeries) |     |     |
| -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | --- | --- |

Inter-operability with IGMP

Static multicast route does not require IGMP to be enabled on routers connected to receivers. Traffic is
flooded on the downstream interface configured for the static multicast route. However, with IGMP and
IGMP snooping enabled, traffic is sent only to the port on which join is received. Static multicast is inter-
operable with both IGMPv2 and IGMPv3.

For an IGMP enabled device, on which IGMPv3 joins are learnt (applicable for MLDv2 as well),
configuring a summarized static route {any, G} causes the traffic not to be forwarded to the clients
sending IGMPv3 joins. In such cases, static multicast routes must be configured with the specific source,
such as {S,G}, for the traffic to flow. This also ensures that traffic from unwanted source for the same
group is not forwarded to the clients sending IGMPv3 joins.

Inter-operability with other vendors

The AOS-CX multicast static route is a proprietary feature and does not inter-operate with other
vendors. However, the router that is redistributing static multicast routes into PIM domain can be
connected to another vendor device in the upstream that supports standards-based protocols such as
PIM-SM (RFC 4601) or PIM-SSM (RFC 4601). Similarly the AOS-CX Router with static multicast can be
connected to a competitor device supporting RFC 3376 and RFC 2236 to learn IGMP joins from the
multicast clients.

Considerations and Best Practices
Static multicast routes must be configured with care to avoid causing traffic drop or duplicate packets.
The following are considerations and best practices for multicast static routes:

n Static multicast route configuration on network with ECMP on downstream/upstream is not

supported. Refer to Static multicast scenarios for more information.

n Static multicast routes should not be used in networks where duplicate traffic issues are resolved by

PIM solves using Assert/Designated Forwarder.

n It is recommended to configure static multicast routers on all required routers before sending

multicast traffic for configured flows. This is applicable for all modes. Momentary traffic drop or
duplicate packets may occur if static multicast routes are configured on the fly on networks where
multicast traffic is already flowing.

n Static multicast routes is not a substitute for PIM. It is recommended for configuration on smaller

networks as it can cause administrative overhead. It must be configured only when there is a scope
of summarization to resolve high resource usage, or in cases where it can solve PIM join/leave
latency issues. Refer to Use cases for more information.

o Refer to Static multicast scenarios for supported scenarios when both PIM and Static multicast

routes are configured on the network.

n All downstream interfaces should be explicitly configured for a static multicast route where multicast

traffic needs to be forwarded.

n When static multicast routes are configured to redistribute into PIM domain, the device elected as RP

on a PIM network should not be configured as the redistributing router.

n With static multicast routes, traffic is not guaranteed to flow in the shortest path tree. Instead, it

follows the path configured using the static multicast routes on each device in the network. Refer to
Static multicast scenarios for more information.

Static routes | 1139

n When static multicast routes are redistributed into PIM, a PIM Join corresponding to the configured
static multicast route is sent on the RPF interface regardless of the configured upstream interface.
PIM Joins are sent periodically based on the configured PIM join/prune interval.

n Static multicast routes are not automatically deleted with traffic inactivity and should be explicitly

removed if they are no longer in use for optimal resource usage.

n Static multicast routes are not supported with VxLAN and GRE tunnels as upstream/downstream

interfaces.

n It is recommended to keep number of static multicast routes on a device limited.

n Some deployments might already be using ACLs or policies to null multicast traffic for specific groups
or sources. If a static multicast route is also configured to drop/allow traffic along with these policies,
traffic is forwarded/routed only if both static multicast route and policy are configured to allow
traffic. If at least one of these configurations is applied to drop the traffic, traffic is dropped.

Use cases
The use cases provide examples of networks using multicast static routes.

Use case 1: SSDP enabled networks

The Simple Service Discovery Protocol (SSDP) is an application layer protocol and one of the key
protocols that implement Universal Plug and Play (UPnP). SSDP enables network devices to discover and
advertise network services by sending multicast discovery and advertisement messages to multicast
IPv4 group address 239.255.255.250:1900 or multicast IPv6 group address FF0x::C[1]. With UPnP, each
device generates a unique multicast flow (Source IP, SSDP Group IP). In a multicast network with a large
number of end user devices, this can bloat up the multicast hardware and software resources as each
device creates a unique (S, G) flow and the resources are limited. In networks, where there is a need to
control, drop, or minimize SSDP traffic, summarized static multicast routes can be configured to save
network resources and to avoid denial of services.

Figure 1 Sample Static multicast route on an SSDP enabled network

Use case 2: Fixed channel subscription scenarios

Static multicast routing can be used to avoid interruptions and delay due to mroute updates in cases
where there is a fixed source and set of clients, such as video conferencing. Static mroute works for a
fixed path through which the route is configured on all the devices or by redistributing static multicast

AOS-CX 10.12 Multicast Guide | (6200, 6300, 6400, 8xxx, 9300, 10000 Switch Series)

1140

routes into PIM domain. The static multicast route should be promptly removed by the user once the
multicast traffic is no longrt needed by the clients. In such cases, static multicast routes should be
configured for a smaller-scale network within a single multicast administrative domain for ease of
management.

Use case 3: IP video surveillance

IP multicast video surveillance deployments are dictated by requirements such as fast convergence,
fault tolerance, and scalability. If the path between the surveillance cameras (multicast source) and the
recorders or storage systems (multicast receivers) is fixed, configuring static multicast route provides
stability and improved convergence time. Since the topology for IP video surveillance networks seldom
changes, end-to-end static multicast routes configuration for the required flows can prevent issues like
surveillance blackout caused by higher convergence time or join/leave latency caused by PIM.

Figure 1 Static multicast route end-to-end and redistribution for IP video surveillance

Use case 4: Precision time protocol

In the multicast transport mode, precision time protocol (PTP) uses dedicated multicast destination IP
address 224.0.1.129 as per IEEE 1588 standards for communication between devices. The primary clock
transmits the clock synchronization messages to the PTP clients as multicast packets. The number of
PTP sources and clients can be large, which can cause a large number of hardware resources to be used
up in the multicast routers which forward the PTP control packets. In networks where there is a need to
optimize the hardware resource usage, summarized static multicast routes can be configured to save
network resources and to avoid delay caused by control plane overheads.

6410 distro-primary-static configuration

In this use case, PIM active active is enabled, static multicast route [S,G] is configured for both ipv4 and
ipv6, and no redistribute is configured on Distro as Redistribute is configured on CORE. VLAN 3005
and VLAN 3006 are client connected VLANs.

Edge-core 1 static configuration

In this use case, PIM is enabled, RP and BSR candidate is configured on this DUT, no static multicast
route is configured, multicast source is connected VLAN 3600, and downstream is LAG with VSX active
forwarding configured.

6410 distro-primary configuration

In this use case, PIM active active is enabled, static multicast route [S,G] is configured for both IPv4 and
IPv6, redistribute the static into PIM as CORE and EDGE-CORE are enabled with only PIM and no static
multicast route is configured on them. VLAN 3005 and VLAN 3006 are client connected VLANs.

vlan 50
vlan 3005

vsx-sync
ip igmp snooping enable
ipv6 mld snooping enable

vlan 3006

vsx-sync
description uplink-6300-standalone-right
ip igmp snooping enable
ipv6 mld snooping enable

interface lag 50 multi-chassis

description uplink-8400
no shutdown
no routing
vlan trunk native 1
vlan trunk allowed 50
lacp mode active

interface lag 90 multi-chassis

no shutdown
no routing
vlan trunk native 1
vlan trunk allowed 3005
lacp mode active

interface lag 91 multi-chassis

description uplink-6300-standalone-right
no shutdown
no routing
vlan trunk native 1
vlan trunk allowed 3006
lacp mode active

interface lag 255

description ISL-1/5/48-To-1/5/48
no shutdown
no routing
vlan trunk native 1
vlan trunk allowed all
lacp mode active

interface vlan 50

vsx active-forwarding
ip address 50.1.1.1/24
ipv6 address 50::/64
ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0
no ip ospf passive
ipv6 ospfv3 1 area 0.0.0.0
ip pim-sparse enable
ipv6 pim6-sparse enable

interface vlan 3005

description uplink-6300-vsf
vsx active-forwarding
ip address 31.1.1.2/24

6410 distro-secondary configuration

In this use case, PIM active active is enabled, static multicast route [S,G] is configured for both IPv4 and
IPv6, redistribute the static into PIM as CORE and EDGE-CORE are enabled with only PIM and no static
multicast route is configured on them. VLAN 3005 and VLAN 3006 are client connected VLANs.

vlan 50
vlan 3005

name 6300-SA-Rig-Acc-Uplink
vsx-sync
ip igmp snooping enable
ipv6 mld snooping enable

vlan 3006

name 6300-VSF-Rig-Acc-Uplin
vsx-sync
description uplink-6300-standalone-right
ip igmp snooping enable
ipv6 mld snooping enable

interface lag 50 multi-chassis

description uplink-8400
no shutdown
no routing
vlan trunk native 1
vlan trunk allowed 50
lacp mode active

interface lag 90 multi-chassis

no shutdown
no routing
vlan trunk native 1
vlan trunk allowed 3005,3007
lacp mode active

interface lag 91 multi-chassis

description uplink-6300-standalone-right
no shutdown
no routing
vlan trunk native 1
vlan trunk allowed 1030,3006
lacp mode active

interface lag 255

description ISL-1/5/48-To-1/5/48
no shutdown
no routing
vlan trunk native 1
vlan trunk allowed all
lacp mode active

interface vlan 50

8360-Edge-Core2#
| Static | Route | commands |     |
| ------ | ----- | -------- | --- |
1242
| AOS-CX10.12MulticastGuide| |     | (6200,6300,6400,8xxx,9300,10000SwitchSeries) |     |
| -------------------------- | --- | -------------------------------------------- | --- |

ip multicast-static-route
ip multicast-static-route <INCOMING-INTERFACE-NAME> {<SOURCE-ADDRESS | ANY>} <GROUP-
ADDRESS> [<OUTGOING-INTERFACE-NAME>] vrf <VRF-NAME>
no ip multicast-static-route <INCOMING-INTERFACE-NAME> {<SOURCE-ADDRESS | ANY>} <GROUP-
ADDRESS> [<OUTGOING-INTERFACE-NAME>] vrf <VRF-NAME>

Description

Configures a multicast static route for (source, group) on an incoming interface with outgoing interface
(s) not involving L3VNI. If a vrf is not specified, default vrf is used.

The no form of this command removes the configuration.

Parameter

Description

<INCOMING-INTERFACE-NAME>

Specifies the multicast stream incoming interface name.

<SOURCE-ADDRESS>

Selects the IPv4 source address.

<ANY>

Selects any source address.

<GROUP-ADDRESS>

Specifies the IPv4 multicast group address.

<OUTGOING-INTERFACE-NAME>

Specifies the outgoing interface name.

vrf <VRF-NAME>

Configures the specified VRF. The default is default vrf.

Usage

If the incoming interface is attached to different vrf than the vrf spcified in the multicast static route
command, route will be made inactive. If the vrf mismatch occurs for the outgoing interface, that
particular outgoing interface will be made inactive. If no outgoing interface is specified, the multicast
traffic is not routed and is bridged on the interface (SVI) on which the traffic is received.

Examples

Configuring Multicast Static Route with a outgoing interface:

switch(config)# ip multicast-static-route vlan10 10.10.1.2 239.255.255.250 vlan20

Removing the configured Multicast Static Route with a outgoing interface:

switch(config)# no ip multicast-static-route vlan10 10.10.1.2 239.255.255.250
vlan20

Configuring Multicast Static Route without an outgoing interface to bridge traffic only in incoming
interface in default vrf:

switch(config)# ip multicast-static-route vlan30 30.30.1.2 239.255.255.250

Static routes | 1243

Removing the configured Multicast Static Route without an outgoing interface to bridge traffic only in
incoming interface in default vrf:

switch(config)# no ip multicast-static-route vlan30 30.30.1.2 239.255.255.250

Configuring Multicast Static Route without an outgoing interface to bridge traffic only in incoming
interface in non-default vrf:

switch(config)# ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan30 30.30.1.2 239.255.255.250 vrf
red

Removing the configured Multicast Static Route without an outgoing interface to bridge traffic only in
incoming interface in non-default vrf:

switch(config)# ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan30 30.30.1.2 239.255.255.250 vrf
red

Configuring Multicast Static Route with multiple outgoing interfaces:

switch(config)# ip multicast-static-route vlan40 40.40.1.2 239.255.255.250 vlan50
vrf red
switch(config)# ip multicast-static-route vlan40 40.40.1.2 239.255.255.250 vlan60
vrf red
switch(config)# ip multicast-static-route vlan40 40.40.1.2 239.255.255.250 1/1/1
vrf red

Removing configured Multicast Static Route with multiple outgoing interfaces:

switch(config)# no ip multicast-static-route vlan40 40.40.1.2 239.255.255.250
vlan60 vrf red
switch(config)# no ip multicast-static-route vlan40 40.40.1.2 239.255.255.250 vrf
red

Configuring Static (\*, Group) Multicast Route with multiple outgoing interfaces:

switch(config)# ip multicast-static-route vlan50 any 239.255.255.250 vlan70 vrf
red
switch(config)# ip multicast-static-route vlan50 any 239.255.255.250 vlan80 vrf
red

Removing configured Static (\*, Group) Multicast Route with multiple outgoing interfaces:

switch(config)# no ip multicast-static-route vlan50 any 239.255.255.250 vlan70 vrf
red
switch(config)# no ip multicast-static-route vlan50 any 239.255.255.250 vlan80 vrf
red

Description

1246
| AOS-CX10.12MulticastGuide| | (6200,6300,6400,8xxx,9300,10000SwitchSeries) |     |     |
| -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | --- | --- |

Parameter

Description

<SOURCE-ADDRESS>

Selects the IPv6 source address.

<ANY>

Selects any source address.

<GROUP-ADDRESS>

Specifies the IPv6 multicast group address.

<OUTGOING-INTERFACE-NAME>

Specifies the outgoing interface name.

vrf <VRF-NAME>

Configures the specified VRF. The default is default vrf.

Usage

If the incoming interface is attached to different vrf than the vrf spcified in the multicast static route
command, route will be made inactive. If the vrf mismatch occurs for the outgoing interface, that
particular outgoing interface will be made inactive.

Examples

Configuring Multicast Static Route with a outgoing interface:

switch(config)# ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan10 2001::1 ff02::c vlan20

Removing the configured Multicast Static Route with a outgoing interface:

switch(config)# no ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan10 2001::1 ff02::c vlan20

Configuring Static (\*, Group) Multicast Route with multiple outgoing interfaces:

switch(config)# ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan50 any ff0e::c vlan70 vrf red
switch(config)# ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan50 any ff0e::c vlan80 vrf red

Removing configured Static (\*, Group) Multicast Route with multiple outgoing interfaces:

switch(config)# no ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan50 any ff0e::c vlan70 vrf red
switch(config)# no ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan50 any ff0e::c vlan80 vrf red

thiscommanddisablestheredistribution.
Usage
Staticmulticastroutesgivetheflexibilitytoprogramaspecificpathformulticasttrafficfromthesource
totheclientwithouthavingtorelyontheunderlyingprotocolstobuildamulticastroute.Theycanbe
configuredonalltheroutersinthepathoronaspecificsectionofrouters.Theremainingsectionof
routerscanbeconfiguredtorunthenativePIMprotocol.Insuchcases,thestaticmulticastrouteis
exportedtoPIMdomainasPIMjoinsusingthiscommand.

| show ip multicst-static-route  |     |     | [all | vrfs | | vrf <VRF-NAME>] |
Description
Displaysthemulticaststaticrouteandcorrespondingsummarizedrouteinformationforthespecified
VRF.IfVRFisnotspecified,thedefaultVRFisdisplayed.
| Parameter |     |     |     | Description              |     |
| --------- | --- | --- | --- | ------------------------ | --- |
| [allvrfs] |     |     |     | SelectsallVRFstodisplay. |     |
[vrf<VRF-NAME>] SpecifiestheVRF todisplay.Thedefaultisdefaultvrf.
Examples
Displayingthemulticaststaticrouteandcorrespondingsummarizedrouteinformationforallvrfs:
| switch#         | show ip    | multicast-static-route |     |                   | all-vrfs |
| --------------- | ---------- | ---------------------- | --- | ----------------- | -------- |
| VRF : red       |            |                        |     |                   |          |
| Group Address   |            |                        | :   | 239.255.255.250   |          |
| Source Address  |            |                        | :   | 40.40.40.2        |          |
| Route type      |            |                        | :   | Static            |          |
| Incoming        | interface  |                        | :   | 1/1/2             |          |
| Outgoing        | Interface  | List                   | :   |                   |          |
| Interface       | State      |                        |     |                   |          |
| ---------       | -----      |                        |     |                   |          |
| vlan10          | forwarding |                        |     |                   |          |
| VRF : blue      |            |                        |     |                   |          |
| Group Address   |            |                        | :   | 239.255.255.250   |          |
| Source Address  |            |                        | :   | Any               |          |
| Route type      |            |                        | :   | Static-Summarized |          |
| Incoming        | interface  |                        | :   | 1/1/3             |          |
| Outgoing        | Interface  | List                   | :   |                   |          |
| Interface       | State      |                        |     |                   |          |
| ---------       | -----      |                        |     |                   |          |
| vlan20          | forwarding |                        |     |                   |          |
| vlan30          | forwarding |                        |     |                   |          |
| Command History |            |                        |     |                   |          |
| Release         |            |                        |     | Modification      |          |

show ip multicst-static-route <GROUP-ADDRESS> {<SOURCE-ADDRESS | ANY>} detail [all vrfs |
vrf <VRF-NAME>]
Description
Displaysthemulticaststaticrouteandcorrespondingsummarizedrouteinformationforthegiven
groupaddressinthegivenVRFdetail.IfVRFisnotspecified,thedefaultVRFisdisplayed.
| Parameter        |     |     |     |     | Description                |
| ---------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | -------------------------- |
| <GROUP-ADDRESS>  |     |     |     |     | Specifiesthegroupaddress.  |
| <SOURCE-ADDRESS> |     |     |     |     | Specifiesthesourceaddress. |
| <ANY>            |     |     |     |     | Selectsanysourceaddress.   |
| [allvrfs]        |     |     |     |     | SelectsallVRFstodisplay.   |
[vrf<VRF-NAME>] SpecifiestheVRF todisplay.Thedefaultisdefaultvrf.
Examples
Displayingthemulticaststaticrouteinformationforaspecificgroup:
switch# show ip multicast-static-route 239.255.255.250 40.40.40.3 detail vrf red
| VRF       | : red     |            |      |          |                 |
| --------- | --------- | ---------- | ---- | -------- | --------------- |
| Group     | Address   |            |      | :        | 239.255.255.250 |
| Source    | Address   |            |      | :        | 40.40.40.2      |
| Route     | type      |            |      | :        | Static          |
| Incoming  | interface |            |      | :        | 1/1/2           |
| Outgoing  | Interface |            | List | :        |                 |
| Interface |           | State      |      | Vteps    |                 |
| --------- |           | -----      |      | -----    |                 |
| vni1000   |           | forwarding |      | 2.2.2.2, | 3.3.3.3         |
Displayingthemulticaststaticrouteinformationforanygrouponvrfred:
Staticroutes|1253

switch# show ip multicast-static-route 239.255.255.250 any detail vrf red

| --------- | ----------- | ---------- | ------- | -------- | ------------------ |

| Source    | Address     |            |         | :        | Any                |
| Route     | type        |            |         | :        | Static             |
| Incoming  | interface   |            |         | :        | 1/1/2              |
| Outgoing  | Interface   |            | List    | :        |                    |
| Interface |             | State      |         | Vteps    |                    |
| --------- |             | -----      |         | -----    |                    |
| vni1000   |             | forwarding |         | 2.2.2.2, | 3.3.3.3            |

| Platforms |             | Command    | context |          | Authority          |

Displayingthemulticaststaticrouteinformationforallvrfs:

| vlan30 forwarding  |      |                     |     |

Displaystherunningconfiguration.
Examples
Displayingtherunningconfiguration:
| switch# | show running-config |     |     |     |     |     |

Therecommendedstaticmulticastroutetroubleshootingflowisprovidedin Figure1,Staticmulticast

Figure1 Staticmulticastroutetroubleshootingflow
1262
| AOS-CX10.12MulticastGuide| | (6200,6300,6400,8xxx,9300,10000SwitchSeries) |     |     |     |     |     |
| -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

Step 1: Check the network reachability between Source DR and the multicast receivers.

a. Ensure there is connectivity between Source DR and Client DR devices where static multicast

routes are configured.

b. Since static multicast routes use L3 interfaces to forward the traffic, the IPv4 or IPv6 address has

to be configured.

c.

It is not mandatory to run unicast routing protocols between Source DR and Client DR devices to
achieve static multicast routing.

Step 2: Validate static multicast configuration on all the routers from Source DR to
Client DR.

a. Ensure there are static multicast routes configured on all devices between Source DR and Client

DR devices so that they form a forwarding path.

switch# show running-config
Current configuration:
!
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ip multicast-static-route vlan10 10.10.1.2 239.255.255.250 1/1/2
ip multicast-static-route 1/1/1 10.10.1.2 239.255.255.250 1/1/2

| Frequently |             | Asked           | Questions |                |     |
Staticroutes|1265

1. Can I enable both PIM and Static Multicast Route for the same flow in a network?
No,ifastaticmulticastrouteisconfiguredandoperational,PIMdoesnotlearnthematchingflow,and
hencedoesnotproduceanmrouteforthisflow.Oncestaticmulticastrouteisdeleted,PIMstarts
learningthisflowandprogramsmrouteintotheforwardingtable.
2. Does Static Multicast Route rely on the underlying unicast routing protocol?
Staticmulticastroutesdonotrelyontheunderlyingunicastroutingprotocolstobeoperational.They
relyontheconfiguredpathtoforwardthetrafficfrommulticastsourcetoreceivers.Forexample,ifthe
networkonlyrequiresmulticastforwardingusingstaticmulticastrouting,OSPF/BGPforunicasttraffic
forwardingisnotrequired.Currently,staticmulticastroutedonotuseRPFchecksorensurereachability
toSourcebeforeprogramminganmrouteintotheforwardingtable.
| 3. Are there | are any | platform | differences | to be aware | of? |
| ------------ | ------- | -------- | ----------- | ----------- | --- |
StaticMulticastRoutingissupportedonthe6200,6300,6400,8100,8320,8325,8360,8400and10000
platforms.However,summarizedstaticmulticastrouteisnotsupportedonthe8400platform.
| 4. Is the | Active-Active | command | required | on VSX deployments? |     |
| --------- | ------------- | ------- | -------- | ------------------- | --- |
StaticmulticastroutefeatureisagnostictoActive-ActiveconfigurationinVSXdevices.Withstatic
multicastroute,VSX-primaryroutesthepacketwhileVSX-Secondaryprogramsabridgeentryinthe
forwardingtableirrespectiveofactive-activeconfiguration.
| 5. Does | the solution | work for | all types | of interfaces? |     |
| ------- | ------------ | -------- | --------- | -------------- | --- |
ThesolutionworkswithalltypesofunderlayinterfacesthatarecurrentlysupportedinAOS-CX.Static
multicastonVxLANandGREtunnelsarenotsupported.
6. Can I replace PIM with Static Multicast route on my network?
No.StaticmulticastrouteisnotasubstituteforPIM.Itshouldbeusedinsmallnetworksunderasingle
administrativedomainandforveryspecificcasesasmentionedinUsecases.
7. What is the difference between static multicast routing as described in this chapter
| versus the | following   | command? |     |     |     |
| ---------- | ----------- | -------- | --- | --- | --- |
| switch#    | router      | pim      |     |     |     |
| ip mroute  | 10.1.1.1/32 | 10.2.2.2 |     |     |     |
Therouter pimcommandconfiguresmulticastroutingtouseaseparatepathforRPF,whichis
differentfromunicastrouting.Itstillrequiresamulticastprotocol,likePIM-SM,toroutethepacket.
Staticmulticastroutingasdescribedinthischapterprogrammulticastforwardingdirectlyintothe
hardwarewithoutPIM.
8. How can I avoid flooding when static multicast routing is defined?
ConfigureIGMPSnoopingatthemutlicastreceivernetwork.

Aruba is committed to providing our customers with information about the chemical substances in our

Documentation Feedback
Aruba is committed to providing documentation that meets your needs. To help us improve the
documentation, send any errors, suggestions, or comments to Documentation Feedback (docsfeedback-
switching@hpe.com). When submitting your feedback, include the document title, part number, edition,
and publication date located on the front cover of the document. For online help content, include the
product name, product version, help edition, and publication date located on the legal notices page.
